paragraphs.content: All I ever wanted was for Juli Baker to just leave me alone. I wanted her to stop bothering me and give me some space. This started in the summer before second grade, when we moved to her neighborhood. Now, we're almost done with eighth grade, and I've been avoiding her for more than five years because she makes me uncomfortable. She didn't just come into my life quietly. She forced her way in very boldly. Did we ask her to climb into our moving van and mess with our boxes? No! But she did it anyway, taking over and showing off like she always does. My dad tried to stop her. He said, 'Hey! What are you doing? You're getting mud everywhere!' because her shoes were really dirty. But she didn’t leave. Instead, she sat down and started pushing a box with her feet. She looked at me and said, 'Don’t you want some help? It looks like you need it.' I didn’t like what she meant by that. Even my dad, who had been giving me similar looks all week, didn’t like her. He said, 'Hey! Don’t do that. There are valuable things in that box.' Then she moved to another box and said, 'We should push this one together!' My dad said, 'No, no, no!' and pulled her up by the arm. He told her to go home because her mom was probably wondering where she was. This was when I realized that Juli doesn’t get hints. When asked to leave, she didn’t go home. Instead, she said, 'Oh, my mom knows where I am.'

She said everything was okay. Then she pointed across the street and said, 'We live right over there.' My dad looked where she was pointing and said quietly, 'Oh boy.' Then he looked at me and winked, saying, 'Bryce, isn't it time for you to go inside and help your mom?' I realized immediately that he was trying to get me to leave. I hadn't thought about it until later, but I had never avoided someone with my dad before. Usually, parents don't tell you it's okay to avoid someone, no matter how annoying they are. But my dad was suggesting it now, and I quickly agreed. I smiled and said, 'Sure thing!' Then I jumped down and went towards our new house. I heard her following me, but I couldn't believe it. It seemed like she was chasing me; maybe she was actually going somewhere else. But before I could turn around to check, she rushed past me, grabbed my arm, and pulled me with her. This was too much. I stopped and was about to tell her to leave me alone when something strange happened. As I was trying to get free, my hand somehow got tangled with hers. Suddenly, I was holding her hand! I tried to let go, but she held on tightly and kept pulling me, saying, 'Come on!' My mom came outside and looked very happy. 'Well, hello,' she said to Juli. 'Hi!' I was still trying to get free, but the girl was holding me very tightly. My mom was smiling, looking at our hands and my red face. 'And what's your name, dear?' 'Julianna Baker. I live right over there,' she replied, pointing with her free hand. 'Well, I see you've met my son,' my mom said, still smiling. 'Uh-huh!' Finally, I got free and did the only brave thing I could think of as a seven-year-old — I hid behind my mom.

Mom hugs me and says, 'Bryce, honey, can you show Julianna our house?' I try to signal to her with my body that I don't want to, but she ignores me and tells me to go ahead. Juli would have walked right in, but Mom noticed her shoes were dirty and asked her to take them off. Then she saw Juli's socks were dirty too, so she had to take those off as well. Juli wasn't shy about it. She just took them off and left them outside. I didn't really show her around. Instead, I locked myself in the bathroom. I yelled to her that I wasn't coming out, and after a while, it got quiet outside. When I finally looked, Juli was gone. I quietly checked and saw she had left. It wasn't the best way to avoid someone, but I was only seven years old. However, Juli kept coming back every day. She would ask if I could play while I hid behind the sofa. Sometimes she even peeked through my window. I had to hide under my bed once when I saw her. Juli doesn't really understand personal space or privacy. She thinks the whole world is her playground. Luckily, my dad helped me by telling her I was busy or asleep or not at home. He really helped me out. My sister, Lynetta, on the other hand, always tried to cause trouble for me. She's four years older and I've learned what not to do by watching her. She seems to start arguments just by being there.

I used to fight a lot with her, but I realized it's not worth it. Girls fight in a tricky way. They pull your hair, scratch you, and pinch you. Then they tell their mom when you try to fight back. After that, you end up in time-out. So, the best thing is to not react to their teasing. Just ignore it and they'll stop eventually. That's how it works with my sister Lynetta. I learned that this method works with everyone, like teachers and even our parents. Arguing with parents is useless, so it's better to just stay out of trouble. Lynetta still doesn't know how to avoid arguments with our parents. She gets too upset and can't calm down. And she thinks I'm the foolish one. Lynetta tried to annoy me with our cousin Juli when she first came. She even managed to sneak her past our dad and looked for me all over the house. I hid on the top shelf of my closet and they didn't see me. Later, I heard dad telling Juli to stay off the old furniture and she had to leave. I didn't go outside much that first week. I just helped with unpacking, watched TV, and stayed inside while my parents decided where to put the furniture.

I really wanted to go outside. But every time I looked out the window, I saw Juli in her yard. She was playing with a soccer ball, kicking it or dribbling it around. When she wasn't playing, she sat on the curb, looking at our house with the ball at her feet. My mom didn't understand why I didn't like that Juli had held my hand once. She thought I should be friends with her and said, 'I thought you liked soccer. Why don't you play with her?' But I didn't want to, because I knew Juli was trouble, even though I was only seven and a half years old. Juli was really trouble. The first day I went to Mrs. Yelson's second-grade class, Juli was too excited to see me. She ran across the room and jumped on me. Mrs. Yelson called it a 'welcome hug,' but it felt more like a strong tackle. Even though I got away from her, it was too late. Everyone teased me, calling Juli my girlfriend and asking if we were married. They even sang a teasing song when she chased me at recess. My first year in that town was really bad. Third grade was the same, with Juli always following me. Fourth grade was no different. But in fifth grade, I decided to do something about it. I started with a small idea to keep Juli away. I thought it was a good way to tell her she wasn't my type. So, I made a plan. I asked another girl, Shelly Stalls, out.

To really enjoy this story, you need to know that Juli does not like Shelly Stalls. She never liked her, and I don't know why. Shelly is nice, friendly, and has a lot of hair. But Juli disliked her, and I planned to use this fact to solve my problem. I thought Shelly would join us for lunch and hang out with me. If Juli saw us together, I could just stay close to Shelly and everything would work out. However, Shelly took it too seriously and told everyone, including Juli, that we were in love. Soon, Juli and Shelly had a big fight. After that, my friend Garrett, who supported my plan, told Shelly what I was trying to do. He denies it, but I found out he's not very trustworthy when girls are upset. That day, the principal questioned me, but I didn't admit to anything. I just said I was sorry and didn't know what was going on. She eventually let me go. Shelly was upset for days and kept following me, making me feel bad. Everything settled down after a week when Shelly broke up with me and started dating Kyle Larsen. Then Juli began showing interest in me again, and I was back to the beginning. In sixth grade, things changed. I don't remember Juli chasing me, but I remember her smelling me. Yes, smelling. Our teacher, Mr. Mertins, made Juli stick to me like glue.

Mertins seems to be an expert in where people should sit, because he paid a lot of attention to choosing our seats. He even put Juli right next to me. Juli Baker is the type of person who always wants to show off how smart she is. She always raises her hand first, gives very long answers, and her projects are always done early. They are so good that teachers show them as examples of perfect work. She also does extra work which makes her scores even higher. But when Mr. Mertins made Juli sit next to me, it was useful because I could see her perfect answers written beautifully, and I started to get better grades. But then Mr. Mertins changed the seating again, and I ended up sitting in front of Juli. That's when she started to lean forward and smell my hair. She would get really close and keep sniffing. I tried to stop her by moving my chair or using my backpack, but nothing worked. I asked Mr. Mertins to move me, but he said no because he didn't want to mess up the class's balance. So, I had to deal with her sniffing. And because I couldn't see her answers anymore, my grades started to drop, especially in spelling.

Once during a test, Juli was smelling my hair and then she noticed I spelled some words wrong. Many words actually. Suddenly, she stopped smelling and started whispering. At first, I was shocked. Could Juli Baker cheat? Yes, she was helping me by spelling words into my ear. Juli was always sneaky about smelling my hair, which annoyed me because nobody saw her do it. However, she was also sneaky about giving me answers, which I didn't mind. The problem was, I began to rely on her help. I thought, why should I study if I don't need to? But soon, getting all the answers from her made me feel like I owed her something. How can you ask someone to stop helping or smelling you when you owe them? It feels wrong. So, in sixth grade, I felt uncomfortable and unhappy, but I hoped things would change next year. In junior high, we would be in a big school, in different classes. I thought I wouldn't have to deal with Juli Baker anymore. I was really looking forward to it being over.

The first day I saw Bryce Loski, I was very excited. Just seeing him made me feel crazy. It was his eyes. There was something about his eyes. They are blue and with his dark eyelashes, they look amazing. Really beautiful. I have known him for more than six years now. I learned to hide how I felt a long time ago, but in the beginning, it was very hard. I really wanted to be with him. It all started two days before second grade began. I had been looking forward to it for weeks, ever since my mom told me a boy my age was moving into the new house across the street. Soccer camp was over and I was very bored because there was no one to play with. There were other kids, but they were all older. That was fine for my brothers, but I was left alone at home. My mom was there, but she was busy. She said she had better things to do than play soccer. I didn't think anything was more fun than playing soccer. But my mom thought doing laundry, dishes, or vacuuming was more important. She didn't want me playing soccer inside while helping her with chores. So, I stayed outside for weeks, hoping the new neighbors would come early. I played soccer with our dog, Champ, for weeks. He mostly just blocked the ball because dogs can't really kick. Sometimes he would push it with his nose. But he would try to bite it and then I would get the ball back. When the Loskis' moving truck finally came, my family was happy. They thought I would finally have a friend to play with.

My mom, who is very wise, made me wait for more than an hour before I could go meet him. 'Let them relax a bit, Julianna,' she said. 'They need some time to get used to the new place.' She didn't even allow me to watch from the yard. 'I know what will happen,' she said. 'Your ball will end up in their yard and you'll go after it.' So, I watched from the window instead, and kept asking her every few minutes, 'Can I go now?' and she would answer, 'Wait a little longer, okay?' Then the phone rang. When I saw my mom was busy, I pulled on her sleeve and asked again, 'Now?' She nodded and said quietly, 'Okay, but be calm! I'll come out soon.' I was so excited that I ran across the street, but I tried to act polite when I reached the moving truck. I waited outside the truck for a very long time, which was difficult because I saw him inside! He was my new neighbor, Bryce Loski. Bryce wasn't really busy. He just watched his dad load boxes onto the truck. I felt bad for Mr. Loski because he looked very tired doing all the work alone. Bryce and his dad were wearing the same turquoise shirts, which I thought was adorable. I couldn't wait anymore, so I shouted, 'Hi!' into the truck. Bryce got scared, then quickly started to move a box, pretending he had been working the whole time. I could tell Bryce felt guilty for not helping much. He probably had been moving stuff for days and was tired. He looked like he needed a break. But Mr. Loski seemed like he wouldn't stop working until he was too tired, and I worried Bryce might get too tired even to enjoy his new home.

The sad event made me jump into the moving van because I needed to help. I wanted to save him! When I reached him to help push a box, he was so tired that he stepped aside and let me do it. Mr. Loski didn't want me to help, but I managed to help Bryce anyway. I was only in the van for three minutes when his dad told him to go inside and help his mom unpack. I ran after Bryce up the path, and that's when things changed. I caught up to him and grabbed his arm, hoping we could play a bit before he had to go inside, but suddenly he was holding my hand and looking into my eyes. My heart stopped. It really stopped. And I felt something I had never felt before. It felt like everything was moving around me, under me, inside me, and I was floating. Floating in the air. And looking into his eyes was the only thing keeping me from floating away. His eyes seemed to connect to mine with an invisible force, holding me there while everything else just disappeared. I almost had my first kiss that day. I think I would have, but then his mom came outside and he got so embarrassed that his face turned red, and he ran to hide in the bathroom. I was waiting for him when his sister, Lynetta, saw me. She looked big and grown-up, and she asked what was happening, so I told her a bit. But I shouldn't have because she started teasing Bryce by calling through the bathroom door, saying things like, 'Hey, baby brother! There's a cute girl out here! Are you scared she has cooties?' It was so embarrassing! I tried to make her stop by pulling her arm, but she didn't stop, so I just left.

I saw my mom outside talking to Mrs. Loski. Mom had given her our lemon cake that we were going to eat after dinner. The cake had white sugar on top and was still warm, making the air smell like sweet lemons. I really wanted to eat that cake, but Mrs. Loski had it now, and I couldn't get it back. All I could do was smell it while they talked about stores and the weather. Later, Mom and I went back home. It felt weird because I didn't get to play with Bryce. All I knew about him was that he had very blue eyes, a sister who was tricky, and that he almost kissed me. That night, I fell asleep thinking about the kiss that didn't happen. I wondered what a kiss felt like. I knew it wouldn't feel like the kisses from Mom or Dad. It would be very different. I remember in second grade, I wanted to kiss him because of his blue eyes and maybe a bit because I was curious. In second and third grade, I always wanted to be near him. By fourth grade, I still liked him a lot, but I didn't run after him anymore. I just watched him and dreamed about him.

Then, when I was in fifth grade, Shelly Stalls joined our school. Shelly Stalls is very annoying. She often complains, spreads rumors, and is not honest, telling different things to different people. Now that we are in junior high, she is known for creating a lot of drama, but she started acting this way even in elementary school. She always avoided physical education class. She never ran or did any exercises. Instead, she pretended to be too weak, saying she would get too tired if she had to run, jump, or stretch. Every year, she managed to avoid physical activities by bringing a note from home and acting very weak in front of the teachers at the start of the school year. After that, she didn’t have to do anything physically demanding. She didn’t even tidy up her own chair. She only used her mouth muscles a lot, by talking all the time. If talking were a sport in the Olympics, Shelly would win a lot of medals. What really annoyed me wasn’t just that she skipped physical education class, because no one would want her in their team anyway. What annoyed me was that people could see she wasn’t really weak or sick. It was just her hair. She had a lot of hair styled in many different ways. It looked like the fancy tails on merry-go-round horses. Sometimes, she let her hair down and it covered her like a blanket, hiding most of her face except her nose. It would be impossible to play games with her hair covering her like that. I decided to just ignore Shelly, which worked well until halfway through fifth grade when I saw her holding hands with Bryce.

My friend Bryce, who was shy about holding my hand just before we started second grade. He was still too shy to talk to me much, only saying hello. He was the first boy I ever kissed. I couldn't understand how Shelly managed to hold his hand. She was too bossy and shouldn't have been clinging to him like that! As they walked, Bryce kept looking back at me. At first, I thought he was apologizing with his eyes. Then I realized he needed my help to get away from her. Shelly was too sensitive and dramatic; if Bryce tried to leave her, she would cry and make a scene. This was something a boy might find hard to handle nicely. It was a job for a girl like me. I didn't look for anyone else to help; I quickly got her away from him. Bryce ran off as soon as he was free, but Shelly didn't run. She attacked me, scratching and pulling at me, insisting that Bryce was hers. I managed to hold her in a headlock and keep her arm twisted until a teacher came. In the end, Shelly went home with messy hair, and I explained everything to the principal. Mrs. Shultz, the principal, is a strong woman who seemed to understand the situation. She told me it's usually better to let others handle their problems, but she was glad I only held Shelly back and did nothing worse.

Shelly came back the next day with her hair in many braids. She made everyone talk about me, but I ignored it. Bryce stayed away from her for the whole year. Bryce didn't start holding my hand, but he became nicer to me. In sixth grade, Mr. Mertins made us sit next to each other which helped. I liked sitting next to Bryce. He was kind and would greet me every morning. Sometimes, he looked at me and then quickly looked away, which made me smile because he was shy and cute. We started talking more when Mr. Mertins moved my seat behind Bryce. Mr. Mertins had a rule that if you misspell more than seven words out of twenty-five, you must spend lunch rewriting them. Bryce was scared of this rule. Even though I felt guilty, I whispered the correct spellings to him, hoping we could spend lunch together. His hair smelled like watermelon, and he had soft, blond fuzz on his earlobes which was strange because his hair was black. I thought about asking Mr. Mertins about earlobe fuzz during our evolution class, but I didn't. Instead, I spent the year helping Bryce with spelling, smelling his watermelon-scented hair, and wondering if he would ever kiss me.

When I was in seventh grade, a lot changed, but the biggest change was not at school. It was at home because my Granddad Duncan came to live with us. At first, it was strange because we didn't know him well, except for Mom. Even though Mom has been telling us for a year and a half that he is a great person, he mostly just likes to look out the window of our living room. There isn't much to see there, just the Bakers' front yard, but he sits there a lot, day and night, in his big comfortable chair, looking out. Sure, he does other things like reading books by Tom Clancy, reading the newspaper, doing crossword puzzles, and checking his stocks, but those are just things he does to pass the time. If he had the choice, he would just look out the window all the time until he fell asleep. Mom thinks he looks out the window so much because he misses Grandma, but Granddad has never talked to me about that. In fact, he didn't talk much to me about anything until a few months ago when he read about my friend Juli in the newspaper. Juli Baker didn't become famous in our town's newspaper, the Mayfield Times, because she was extremely smart. She was on the front page because she wouldn't come down from a sycamore tree. I can't tell the difference between a sycamore tree and other trees like maple or birch, but Juli knew it was a sycamore and told everyone about it. This sycamore tree was on a hill in an empty lot on Collier Street, and it was very big and ugly. It looked twisted and bent, and I always thought it would fall over when it was windy.

One day last year, I got really tired of her talking about that silly tree. I finally told her that it wasn't a great sycamore tree but actually the ugliest tree ever. And guess what she said? She called me visually challenged! Can you believe it? Especially when her house looks terrible. It has overgrown bushes, weeds everywhere, and lots of animals like dogs, cats, chickens, and even snakes running around. Her brothers even have a big snake, a boa constrictor, in their room. They once made me watch it eat a rat when I was ten. The rat was alive and they just fed it to the snake, which scared me a lot. Normally, I don't care about how someone's yard looks, but it really bothers my dad. He works hard on our yard to show them how it should be done. While her brothers care for their snake, I have to do extra work like mowing and cleaning our yard. Juli's dad, who is strong and builds things with bricks, doesn't fix their house. Instead, he paints pictures, which he sells, but I don't find them impressive. Every year at the fair, my parents say he should spend more time on their yard. My mom talks to Juli's mom sometimes. My mom thinks Mrs. Baker is unhappy because she married someone who dreams a lot.

Maybe Juli's taste in beauty was ruined by her dad, and it's not her fault. Juli always believed the big tree was a special gift to our area. When she was in third and fourth grade, she would play in the tree with her brothers, climbing and sliding down its trunk. Whenever we drove by, Juli would be hanging from the tree, risking falling, while my mom told me and my sister never to climb it because it was too dangerous. My sister didn't care, and I just hoped Juli wouldn't call out my name. I did try to climb the tree once in fifth grade, after Juli saved my kite from it. She climbed very high and got it, then gave it back nicely. I felt weak because I was too scared to climb up there myself. The next day, I tried to climb higher than Juli, but looking down scared me a lot. I realized I was not good at climbing trees.

When junior high started, I hoped to avoid Juli but couldn't. We both took the same bus. There were eight of us at the bus stop, which helped a little, but not much. Juli always tried to stand or talk to me, which embarrassed me. Then, she began climbing trees. She was in seventh grade, climbing high up and shouting how far away the bus was. She wanted me to climb with her, imagining people teasing us about being in a tree together. One day, she swung from a branch, startling me and causing me to hurt my neck. After that, I avoided waiting under that tree and started leaving for the bus at the last minute from a different spot. This worked well through seventh and most of eighth grade until one day, I noticed a disturbance up the street. Trucks were near our bus stop and people were shouting at Juli, who was up in the tree again. Other kids gathered, and everyone told her to come down. She seemed okay, but I didn't understand the fuss.

I walked up the hill, and when I got closer and saw what the men had, I quickly realized why Juli wouldn't come down from the tree. They had chain saws. Please don't misunderstand. The tree was really twisted and ugly with lots of weird branches. The girl arguing with the men was Juli — she always knows everything and likes to be in charge. Suddenly, I felt sick. Juli really loved that tree. Even though it looked silly, she loved it, and cutting it down would hurt her a lot. Everyone tried to convince her to come down. I tried too. But she said she would never come down, and then she tried to convince us to join her. 'Bryce, please! Come up here with me. They won't cut it down if we're all up here!' I thought about it for a moment. But then the bus came, and I decided not to. It wasn't my tree, and it wasn't really Juli's either. We got on the bus and left her there, but I couldn't focus at school. I kept thinking about Juli. Was she still in the tree? Would they arrest her? When the bus brought us back that afternoon, Juli was gone and so was half the tree. The top branches, where my kite had been stuck and where she liked to sit, were all gone. We watched the men work for a while, the chain saws very loud and smoking as they cut through the tree. The tree looked very odd and bare, and soon I had to leave. It felt like I was watching something very sad, and for the first time in a long time, I felt like crying. Crying over a tree I didn't even like. I went home and tried to forget about it, but I kept wondering if I should have climbed the tree with her. Would it have helped? I thought about calling Juli to say I was sorry about the tree, but I didn't. It would have been too strange. She wasn't at the bus stop the next morning and didn't take the bus home that afternoon either.

That evening, just before dinner, my grandfather asked me to come to the front room. He didn't shout out to me as I passed by, because that would seem too friendly. Instead, he spoke to my mom, who then spoke to me. 'I'm not sure what he wants, dear,' she told me. 'Maybe he wants to start getting to know you better.' Great. He's had a year and a half to get to know me, and now he decides to do it. But I had to go see him anyway. My grandfather is a big man with a large nose and gray-and-black hair slicked back. He always wears house slippers and a jacket, and he's always clean-shaven because he shaves several times a day. He enjoys it a lot. He also has very large hands. You would notice his hands anyway, but his wedding ring makes them look even bigger. That ring is stuck on his finger forever, and even though my mom says that's good, I think it's too tight. If he gains more weight, it might hurt his finger. When I entered the room, he was sitting with his hands together on a newspaper in his lap. I asked, 'Granddad? Did you want to see me?' He replied, 'Have a seat, son.' Son? He hardly ever remembers me, and now he calls me 'son'? I sat down across from him and waited. Then he said, 'Tell me about your friend Juli Baker.' 'Juli? She's not really my friend...' I replied. 'Why not?' he asked calmly, like he already knew something. I started to explain, then stopped and asked, 'Why do you want to know?' He straightened the newspaper and that's when I saw that Juli Baker was on the front page of the Mayfield Times.

There was a big picture of her in the tree with firefighters and policemen around, and some smaller pictures that I couldn't see clearly. 'Can I see that?' He folded it but didn't give it to me. 'Why isn't she your friend, Bryce?' 'Because she's...' I shook my head and said, 'You'd have to know Juli.' 'I'd like to.' 'What? Why?' 'Because the girl is very strong. Why don't you invite her over sometime?' 'Very strong? Granddad, you don't get it! She's really annoying. She always wants to be the center of attention, she thinks she knows everything, and she's too pushy!' 'Really?' 'Yes! And she's been following me around since second grade!' He looked unhappy, then stared out the window and asked, 'Have they lived there that long?' 'I think they were all born there!' He looked unhappy again, then faced me and said, 'Not everyone has a neighbor like her, you know.' 'Lucky them!' He watched me closely. I asked, 'What?' but he just kept looking at me, and I had to look away. Remember, this was the first real talk I had with my grandfather. It was the first time he really tried to talk to me about something other than simple things like passing the salt. And does he want to know me? No! He wants to know about Juli! I couldn't just get up and leave, even though I wanted to. I felt if I left like that, he wouldn't talk to me anymore, even about simple things. So I sat there feeling upset. Was he angry at me? How could he be? I hadn't done anything wrong! When I looked up, he was offering me the newspaper. 'Read this,' he said. 'Without prejudice.' I took it, and when he looked out the window again, I knew - he was done talking.

When I reached my room, I was really angry. I closed my bedroom door loudly and lay on my bed. I was upset about my grandfather for some time, and then I put the newspaper in the lowest drawer of my desk. I didn't want to read more about Juli Baker. At dinner, my mom noticed I was upset and kept looking at me and my grandfather. My grandfather didn't need any salt, which was good because I felt like throwing the salt shaker at him. My sister and dad acted normal. Lynetta barely ate her carrot salad and just picked at her chicken, while my dad talked a lot about work and changes needed there. No one really listened to him — they usually don't when he talks like that — and this time, Mom wasn't even pretending to listen. Mom also didn't try to convince Lynetta that the food was good. She just kept watching me and Granddad, trying to figure out why we were upset with each other. It wasn't like I had done anything to Granddad. Nothing at all. But he seemed upset with me, I could feel it. I avoided looking at him until midway through dinner, when I quickly looked at him. He was watching me closely. It wasn't a mean look, but it was serious and it made me uncomfortable. I wondered what was bothering him. I didn't look at him or my mom again. I just kept eating and pretended to listen to my dad. At the first chance, I left the table and went to my room. I planned to call my friend Garrett like I usually do when I'm upset. I even dialed his number, but then I hung up. Later, when my mom came into my room, I pretended to be asleep. I haven't done that in a long time. The whole night felt strange. I just wanted to be by myself.

Juli wasn't at the bus stop the next morning, or even on Friday. She was at school, but it was hard to tell unless you really looked for her. She wasn't raising her hand eagerly in class or rushing through the halls. She wasn't speaking up without being asked to. I told myself I should be happy she was so quiet — wasn't that what I always wanted? But I still felt sorry for her. I felt sorry about her tree, about how she ate alone in the library, and about her sad eyes. I wanted to say sorry about her tree, but I couldn't find the words. By the middle of the next week, the tree was completely removed. They cleaned the area and tried to remove the stump, but it was too tough and they had to grind it into the ground. Juli still wasn't coming to the bus stop, and by the end of the week, Garrett told me she was biking to school. He saw her fixing her bike's chain on the roadside. I thought she would return to the bus once she got over the tree. I even found myself looking for her. Then, one rainy day, I thought she would come to the bus stop, but she didn't. Garrett saw her biking in a yellow poncho, and her pants were wet in math class. After math, I almost went to suggest she take the bus, but I stopped myself. What if she misunderstood my suggestion? I decided it was best to leave things as they were. The last thing I wanted was for Juli to think I missed her.

I enjoy watching my dad when he paints. I especially like listening to him talk while he paints. His voice is soft and deep when he is painting a picture of a place. He is not sad, just tired and calm. My dad doesn't have a special room to paint in, and our garage is too full of stuff, so he paints outside. The best places to paint are not close to our home. So, my dad keeps a camera in his truck. He works as a mason and travels to different places. He looks for beautiful sunrises, sunsets, or fields with animals. Then he chooses a photo, attaches it to his easel, and paints it. The paintings are good, but I feel a bit sad for him because he has to paint in our backyard, which is not very pretty. Our yard was never great, and it got worse when I started keeping chickens. But when my dad paints, he doesn’t seem to notice the backyard or the chickens. He sees something bigger than just the photo or the canvas. He looks like he is in another world. His strong hands move the small brush so gently over the canvas, it's like he is a different person. When I was younger, my dad would let me sit with him while he painted if I stayed quiet. I am not usually quiet, but I learned that if I was silent for a little while, he would start to tell stories. I learned a lot about my dad this way. He shared stories about his youth, his first job, and how he wished he had finished college.

As I grew up, he continued to talk about his own childhood, but he began to ask me questions too. What were we studying in school? Which book was I reading? What were my thoughts on various topics? One day, he unexpectedly asked me about Bryce. Why did I like Bryce so much? I tried to explain about Bryce's eyes, his hair, and how his cheeks turned red, but I didn't do a good job because my dad just shook his head. He told me gently but seriously that I should look at the bigger picture. I wasn't sure what he meant, and I felt like arguing. How could he know about Bryce without meeting him? However, this wasn't the right place to argue. We usually argued in other parts of the house, not here. We stayed silent for a very long time until he kissed my forehead and mentioned something about 'proper lighting' being crucial. I didn't understand what he meant by 'proper lighting' and was too embarrassed to ask, thinking it would show I wasn't grown up enough. Later, my dad shifted from talking about events to discussing ideas. As I got older, he seemed to talk more about deep, thoughtful topics. I wasn't sure if he was actually becoming more philosophical, or if he thought I was now old enough to understand because I was older. Often, I didn't grasp what he talked about, but sometimes an event would make his words clear. He explained that just like a painting, where a cow is just a cow, a meadow is just grass and flowers, and sunlight through trees is just light, when you combine them, they create something magical. I got what he meant, but I didn't truly feel it until one day I was sitting up in a sycamore tree.

The old sycamore tree stood on top of the hill for a very long time. It was in a large empty space and provided shade during summer and a home for birds in spring. It also had a natural slide. Its trunk curved in a big spiral, making it fun to slide down. My mom believed the tree was hurt when it was young but it grew anyway. She thought it was about a hundred years old and the biggest tree she had ever seen. She said it was a symbol of survival. I always played around the tree, but I started climbing it seriously in fifth grade to get a kite that was stuck in its branches. I saw the kite flying freely before it fell near the sycamore tree. I know kites can be lost forever or sometimes you can get them back. Kites can be lucky or tricky. I've had both kinds, and a lucky kite is worth going after. This kite seemed lucky. It was a simple diamond shape with blue and yellow stripes. It moved in a friendly way and seemed tired when it fell, not mean. Tricky kites fall because they are mean. They don't get tired because they don't fly long. They just crash for fun. So, Champ and I ran up to Collier Street. After looking around, Champ started barking at the sycamore tree. I looked up and saw the kite, with its blue and yellow colors, in the branches. It was high up, but I decided to try and get it. I climbed the trunk, used the slide as a shortcut, and kept climbing. Champ watched and barked, encouraging me. I climbed higher than ever before, but the kite was still far away.
I saw Bryce walking around the corner and into the empty lot. He was looking up, and I knew he was looking for his kite.
This kite was really lucky!
'Can you climb that high?' he shouted to me.
'Yes!' I shouted back and climbed higher and higher!
The tree branches were strong and made it easy for me to climb.
The higher I climbed, the more beautiful the view became. It was like being in an airplane, looking down at houses and trees. It felt like I was on top of the world!

Then I looked down at Bryce and felt dizzy. I felt very high up! Bryce yelled, 'Can you reach the kite?' I took a deep breath and shouted, 'Yes, no problem!' I kept my eyes on the kite's blue and yellow stripes as I climbed higher. Finally, I grabbed the kite!
But the kite's string was stuck in the branches. Bryce yelled, 'Break the string!' and I broke it.
I held the kite and rested for a moment before starting to climb down. I didn't look down; I just enjoyed the view.
I started to feel like I was flying, not scared anymore. It was amazing.
The air smelled wonderful, like sunshine, wild grass, pomegranates, and rain. I kept breathing in the lovely smell.
Bryce called up, 'Are you stuck?' That made me focus on getting down. I held the kite and started to climb down carefully. Bryce walked around the tree, making sure I was safe.

When I slid down, I started feeling excited because Bryce and I were by ourselves. Alone! My heart beat fast as I offered him the kite, but then Champ, the dog, pushed me and I felt his cold nose. On my skin?! I checked my jeans and saw they were torn. Bryce laughed nervously, realizing my pants were ripped, and my face turned very red. He grabbed his kite and left, and I checked the tear in my jeans. I got over the embarrassment, but I couldn't stop thinking about how great it felt to be high up in the tree. I wanted to experience that again and again. Soon, I wasn't scared of heights and found a favorite spot in the tree where I could sit for hours and watch the world. The sunsets were beautiful, sometimes purple and pink, other times bright orange. One day, I truly understood what my dad meant by 'the whole is greater than the sum of its parts' as I looked out from my tree. The view was magical. I felt both humble and grand, peaceful yet full of wonder. How could a simple tree make me feel so many things? I climbed the tree every chance I got, especially in junior high because the school bus stopped right by the tree. I tried to climb as high as possible before the bus arrived, but soon I started going early to watch the sunrise, see birds, or watch other kids gather at the bus stop.

I tried to get the kids at the bus stop to climb the tree with me, but they didn't want to because they would get dirty. I was surprised they didn't want to experience something magical just because of a little dirt. I never told my mom about climbing the tree because she would think it's too risky. My brothers wouldn't care much about it. So, I thought about telling my dad because he might understand, but I was scared he would tell my mom and then they would make me stop climbing. So I kept it to myself and continued climbing, feeling happy yet alone when I looked out from the tree. A few months back, I found myself talking to the tree as if it were a person. When I climbed down, I felt sad because I didn't have a close friend to talk to like other kids. The kids at school weren't really my friends and they wouldn't be interested in climbing trees or enjoying nature. One night, after dinner, my dad went outside to paint under the porch light. He was finishing a painting of a sunrise. I put on my jacket and sat quietly next to him. After a while, he asked me what I was thinking about. He had never asked me that before. I tried to speak but couldn't. He mixed some orange paint and gently encouraged me to talk. I finally told him that I understood why he liked painting outside. He joked and asked if I could explain it to my mom. I told him I realized that some things are better when you put them together.

He stopped what he was doing. 'You do? What happened? Tell me about it!' So I told him about the big tree. I talked about what I could see from the tree, the sounds I heard, the different colors, the feeling of the wind, and how it felt like I was flying. It felt magical. He listened to me without saying anything. When I finished, I looked at him and quietly asked, 'Would you climb the tree with me?' He thought for a while, then smiled and said, 'I'm not very good at climbing anymore, Julianna, but I'll try. How about this weekend when it's nice and bright?' 'Great!' I was so excited that night, I barely slept at all. Saturday was almost here. I was very excited! The next morning, I went to the bus stop very early and climbed the tree. I saw the sunrise, with beautiful colors spreading across the sky. As I was thinking about what I would show my dad, I heard a noise below. I looked down and saw two big trucks under the tree. One truck was pulling a long, empty trailer, and the other had a cherry picker, which is used for fixing things high up like power lines. Four men were standing there, talking and drinking from their bottles. I almost yelled to them that they were parked at a bus stop. But before I could say anything, one of the men started taking out tools from the truck. Gloves, ropes, a chain, earmuffs, and three chain saws. I still didn't understand what they were there to cut down. Then a kid from the bus came and started talking to them. Soon, he pointed up at me.

A man shouted, 'Hey! Come down. We need to remove this.' I held the branch tightly, feeling like I might fall. I asked, 'The tree?' 'Yes, get down now.' 'But who said to cut it?' 'The owner!' he replied. 'Why?' I could see him frown from high up. 'Because he wants to build a house here, and the tree is in the way. Come on, we have to work.' By then, many kids were waiting for the bus. They just looked at me and talked among themselves. Then Bryce came, and I saw the bus coming. I was very scared. I shouted, 'You can't cut it down!' A man said, 'I might call the police. You're in the way of our work. Will you come down or should we cut the tree down with you?' The bus was getting closer. I always go to school, but I felt I would miss the bus today. 'You'll have to cut me down!' I shouted. Then I thought of a plan. 'Hey, everyone!' I called to my friends. 'Come up here! They can't cut the tree if we are all in it!' But they just looked at me. I saw the bus very close. 'Just come up a little. Please!' The bus arrived and stopped. My classmates got on one by one.

After that, things got confusing. I recall neighbors coming together and police using loudspeakers. I remember firefighters, and a man complaining it was his tree and I should leave it. Someone found my mom, who was very upset and didn't act like a calm mother, but I refused to come down. Then, my dad arrived quickly. He talked to my mom, then a man with a cherry picker helped him come up to me. Soon after, I began to cry and wanted him to enjoy the view with me, but he wouldn't. He told me no view was more important than my safety. He helped me down and we went home, but I couldn't handle hearing chainsaws from afar. So, I went with him to his job site, and I stayed in his truck crying. I cried for two weeks. I still went to school and did my best, but I stopped taking the bus. I rode my bike the long way to avoid the place where my favorite tree had been cut down. Then one night, locked in my room, my dad came in with something covered by a towel. It was a painting, as that's how he carries important ones. He revealed it and mentioned he had always liked that tree. I tried to be okay, but he insisted I wouldn't just get over it. I cried, saying it was just a tree. He disagreed, stressing it was more than just a tree to us.

"Please wait a moment," he said and took a deep breath. "I want you to always feel the spirit of that tree. Remember how it felt when you were in it." He paused, then gave me a painting. "I made this for you." I removed the towel covering it, and there was my tree, the big and beautiful sycamore tree. He painted the sunrise through the branches, and it felt like I could feel the wind. In the tree, there was a small girl looking far away, her cheeks red from the wind. From happiness. From magic. "Don't cry, Julianna. I made it to make you feel good, not sad." I wiped my tears and took a deep breath. "Thank you, Daddy," I managed to say. "Thank you." I placed the painting where I could see it from my bed. It's the first thing I see when I wake up and the last thing before I sleep. Now, when I look at it and don't cry, I understand more than just the tree and what it meant to be in its branches. I remember the day I started seeing everything around me differently.

I am afraid of eggs and chickens. You might find this funny, but I am very serious. It all began in sixth grade because of eggs, a snake, and the Baker brothers. The Baker brothers are named Matt and Mike, but I can't tell them apart. They are always together, look alike, sound alike, and are in the same class as Lynetta, maybe because one of them had to repeat a year. I don't think any teacher would want to deal with them for two years. Matt and Mike showed me that snakes can eat eggs whole, with the shell. I wouldn't have known this if it wasn't for Lynetta. Lynetta liked Skyler Brown, who lived nearby and played drums. Skyler and Juli's brothers started a band called Mystery Pisser. When my mom found out the band's name, she was upset and thought it was inappropriate. Lynetta told her it was just a name to shock people. My mom felt old and upset. Lynetta just shrugged, suggesting my mom was overreacting. My mom told Lynetta to go to her room. Lynetta argued she hadn't done anything wrong, but my mom insisted she needed to change her attitude.

Lynetta had to go to a timeout because she was a teenager. After that, whenever Lynetta was late for dinner by two minutes, my mom would send me to Skyler's house to bring her back. It might have been embarrassing for Lynetta, but it was even more embarrassing for me because I was much younger, still in elementary school, and the high school guys at Skyler's house looked tough and acted like rock stars. I was so nervous every time I went there that my voice would squeak when I told Lynetta it was time to eat. Eventually, the high school band changed their name from Mystery Pisser to just Pisser, and they got used to me coming over. They started to be friendly, calling me 'baby brother' and asking me to join them. That's how I ended up in Skyler Brown's garage, hanging out with the older kids and watching a snake eat eggs. I had seen the snake eat a rat before, so I wasn't as shocked this time. I understood they were trying to scare me with the snake, but I didn't want to show that I was scared. Watching a snake eat an egg is actually scarier than it sounds. The snake could open its mouth really wide and just swallowed the eggs whole. We could see the eggs go down its throat. But there was more. After the snake ate three eggs, one of the guys asked me how the snake would digest them. I guessed maybe with stomach acid. He joked that the snake might need a tree or a leg to help digest, and asked if I wanted to volunteer my leg. I stepped back, worried the snake might try to eat my leg next.

He said 'No!' and laughed, pointing at the snake moving across the room. 'Too bad, it's going the other way. It's going to use the piano!' I wondered what type of snake it was and how my sister could stay calm around it. She looked okay, but I knew Lynetta was really scared. The snake coiled around the piano leg three times. Then Matt or Mike told everyone to be quiet. 'Shhh! Here it goes!' he said. The snake stopped and squeezed. We heard the sound of eggs breaking inside the snake. 'Yuck!' the girls screamed. 'Wow!' the boys exclaimed. Mike and Matt grinned at each other and said, 'Time to eat!' I pretended not to be bothered by the snake, but it actually started to give me nightmares about it eating eggs, rats, cats, and even me. Then, things got really scary. Two weeks after seeing the snake at Skyler's place, Juli showed up at our door with a half-carton of eggs. She was very excited, like it was a special holiday. 'Hi Bryce! Remember Abby, Bonnie, Clyde, Dexter, Eunice, and Florence?' I just looked at her. I remembered Santa's reindeer differently. 'You mean my chickens? The ones I raised for last year's science fair?' she asked. 'Oh, right. How could I forget?' 'They're laying eggs now!' She handed me the eggs. 'These are for you and your family.' 'Oh, thanks,' I replied, and shut the door. I used to enjoy eggs, especially scrambled with bacon or sausage. But after the snake incident, these eggs didn't seem appealing at all. They came from the chickens that were once the chicks Juli had hatched for our fifth-grade science fair.

Juli was the star of the fair with her project about watching eggs. There isn't much happening when you're watching eggs hatch. You need a light, a container, some newspaper, and that's all. But Juli wrote a very long report and made lots of charts about eggs. She even timed the eggs to hatch during the fair. I had a volcano at the fair, but everyone liked Juli's hatching chicks more. I thought it was boring. Juli talked a lot to the judges and used a pointer to show her charts. She could have worn a chicken costume, she was that enthusiastic. A year later, I was still annoyed by her project when I got some eggs from her. My mom was excited about the eggs, but I just wanted cereal.

"Grew them?" "Yes, her chickens laid them." "Oh?" She looked surprised and checked the eggs again. "I didn't know she had chickens." "Remember? You and Dad watched them hatch at the science fair last year?" "How do we know there are no chicks inside these eggs?" I said. "I'm just going to eat cereal." We all ate cereal, but we talked about the eggs. Dad said they would be fine because he ate farm eggs as a kid and liked them. Mom was worried about finding a chick inside an egg. Then we talked about roosters and their role, which I didn't care about while eating my Cheerios. Lynetta then said, "If there was a rooster, wouldn't the neighbors know?" We all agreed that was a good point. Then Mom suggested maybe the rooster was made quiet, like how dogs can be made to not bark. Dad thought the idea was silly but agreed with Mom to avoid arguing. "Maybe," he said. Lynetta told Mom, "Just call Mrs. Baker and ask her." Mom replied, "I don't want to be rude and question her eggs." I suggested asking Matt or Mike instead. Lynetta got angry at me and told me to be quiet. I asked why she was upset. Mom was surprised by how Lynetta spoke to me. Lynetta got upset, saying I should have noticed she wasn't visiting there. Mom asked if something happened. Lynetta stormed off to her room. Dad just said, "Oh, boy."

Mom says 'Excuse me' and walks down the hall after Lynetta. When my mom leaves, my dad says, 'So, son, why don't you just ask Juli?' I reply, 'Dad!' He says, 'It's just a small question, Bryce. No problem.' I complain, 'But she'll talk for half an hour!' Dad looks at me and says, 'No boy should be this scared of a girl.' I say, 'I'm not scared of her!' Dad says, 'I think you are.' I reply, 'Dad!' He tells me, 'Really, son. I want you to find out for us. Face your fear and get us an answer.' I ask, 'About whether they have a rooster?' Dad confirms, 'That's right.' He stands up, cleans his cereal bowl, and says, 'I have to go to work and you have to go to school. I'll expect a report tonight.' The day seemed bad from the start. But at school, when I told Garrett what happened, he just shrugged and said, 'Well, she lives right across the street from you, right?' I say, 'Yeah, so?' He suggests, 'So just look over the fence.' I ask, 'You mean spy?' He says, 'Sure.' I wonder, 'But how can I tell if one of them is a rooster or not?' Garrett says, 'Roosters are... I don't know... bigger. And they have more feathers.' I ask, 'Feathers? Like I have to count them?' He replies, 'No, silly! My mom says the male is always brighter.' Then he laughs and says, 'Although in your case I'm not so sure.' I say, 'Thanks. You're really helping me, buddy. I appreciate it.' He explains, 'Look, a rooster will be bigger and have brighter feathers. You know, those long ones on the back? They're redder or blacker or something. And don't roosters have some rubbery red stuff on top of their head? And on their neck, too? Yeah, the rooster has all sorts of rubbery red stuff around its face.' I say, 'So you're telling me to look for big feathers and rubbery red stuff.' He thinks and says, 'Well, chickens have that rubbery red stuff, too. Just not as much.' I roll my eyes at him and was about to say, Forget it, I'll just ask Juli, but then he says, 'I'll come with you if you want.'

"Really?" "Yes, really." And that's why I ended up peeking over the Bakers' fence with Garrett Anderson at three-thirty in the afternoon. It wasn't my first choice, but I had to do it to tell my dad about it later at dinner. We rushed there quickly. The school bell rang and we ran off the school grounds because I thought if we got to the Bakers' soon, we could check things out and leave before Juli got home. We didn't even stop to drop off our backpacks. We went straight to the alley and began spying. It's not really important to peer over the Bakers' fence. You can see almost everything through the fence. But Garrett kept looking over, so I did too, even though I knew I had to live in this neighborhood, not Garrett. The backyard was untidy. No surprise there. The bushes were wild, there was a messy pen made of wood and wire on one side, and instead of grass, there was just very fertile dirt. Garrett spotted their dog first, lying on the patio between two old chairs. He pointed at it and asked, "Do you think he'll bother us?" "We won't be here long enough to have any problems! Where are those silly chickens?" "Probably in that pen," he replied, then threw a rock at the wooden and wire mess. At first, we just heard feathers rustling, but then a bird fluttered out. It didn't go far, but we could see its feathers and some red skin. "So?" I asked him. "Is that a rooster?" He just shrugged. "Looks like a chicken to me." "How can you tell?" He shrugged again. "It just does." We watched it scratch the dirt for a while, then I asked, "What's a hen, anyway?" "A hen?" "Yes. There are roosters, there are chickens, and then there are hens. What's a hen?" "It's one of those," he said, pointing to the Bakers' backyard.

Then he asks, 'What is a chicken?' He looks at me like I'm strange. 'What do you mean?' 'Chickens! What is a chicken?' He steps back and says, 'Brycie boy, you're confused. That's a chicken!' He bends down to pick up another stone, and just as he's about to throw it, the glass door to the backyard opens and Juli comes out. We both hide. While peeking through the fence, I ask, 'When did she come back?' Garrett mutters, 'While you were mixed up about chickens.' Then he whispers, 'But look, this is good. She has a basket, right? She must be here to collect eggs.' First, she has to cuddle with her scruffy dog. She kneels and hugs and pets him, telling him he's a good boy. After she lets him go back to sleep, she stops to talk to a bird Garrett scared away, and then she starts to sing. Singing loudly, she sings, 'I've got sunshine on a cloudy day. When it's cold outside, I've got the month of May. I guess you'd say, what makes me feel this way? My girls. Talking about my little girls...' She looks inside the chicken coop and says, 'Hello, Flo! Good afternoon, Bonnie! Come on out, pumpkin!' The coop is too small for her to walk into. It's like a tiny shelter that even her dog would struggle to get into. Does that stop Juli Baker? No. She gets on her hands and knees and crawls right in. Chickens start running and flying out, and soon the yard is full of birds, and all we can see of Juli are her shoes covered in poop.

We can hear more than just noises. She is singing happily in the chicken coop, saying, 'I don't need money or fame. I am rich with my little girls.' At that moment, I was not looking at the chickens but at Juli Baker's feet, wondering how she could be so happy in an old chicken coop with dirty shoes.

Garrett reminded me to focus. 'They are all chickens,' he said. I stopped looking at Juli's shoes and started looking at the chickens. I counted them to make sure they were all there: one, two, three, four, five, six.
Everyone knew Juli had hatched six chickens, which was a record at our school.

I was confused by what Garrett meant when he said they were all chickens. I didn't understand, but I didn't want him to get upset with me again. Finally, I asked him if there was no rooster. He said, 'Exactly.'
'How can you tell?' I asked. He replied, 'Roosters walk in a proud way.'
'Walk proudly,' I repeated. 'Yes, and none of them have long feathers or much red skin on their heads,' he added. 'They are definitely all chickens.'
That evening, my dad asked directly, 'So, son, did you finish your task?' He was eating pasta as he spoke.
I was eating my pasta too and nodded, 'Yes,' I said proudly. 'They're all chickens.' My dad stopped turning his fork and looked puzzled. 'And…?' he asked.
I sensed something was wrong but kept smiling. 'And what?' I asked. He put down his fork and looked at me seriously. 'Is that what she said? "They're all chickens"?'

"Uh, not exactly." "Then what did she actually say?" "Uh ... she didn't really say anything." "What does that mean?" "It means I went there and checked it out myself." I tried to make it seem like a big deal, but he didn't believe me. "You didn't ask her?" "I didn't need to. Garrett knows a lot about chickens, so we just went and figured it out." Lynetta finished washing her noodles and grabbed the salt, looking annoyed at me, she said, "You're the chicken." "Lynetta!" my mother said. "Be nice." Lynetta stopped using the salt. "Mom, he was spying. He peeked over the fence. Are you okay with that?" My mom looked at me. "Bryce? Is that true?" Now everyone was looking at me, and I felt I needed to defend myself. "What's the problem? You asked me to check on her chickens, and I did!" "Brawk-brawk-brawk!" my sister teased quietly. My dad still wasn't eating. "And what you found out," he said slowly, "is that they're just chickens." "Exactly." He sighed, then finally ate some noodles and chewed them slowly. I felt like I was in trouble, but I didn't know why. So I said, "And you all can eat those eggs, but I'm not going to touch them, so don't ask." My mom looked worried and glanced between my dad and me while eating her salad, waiting for him to comment on my spying. But he stayed quiet, so she asked, "Why not?" "Because there's... well, there's... I don't know how to put this nicely." "Just say it," my dad said sharply. "Well, there's, you know, poop everywhere." "Oh, gross!" my sister exclaimed, dropping her fork. "You mean chicken droppings?" my mom asked.

Yes. There is no grass, only dirt and chicken poop. The chickens walk and peck around in it and..." "Ew, that's disgusting!" Lynetta cries out. "It's true!" Lynetta stands up and says, "You think I can eat after hearing that?" and she leaves the room quickly. "Lynetta! You need to eat something," my mother calls out to her. "No, I don't!" she yells back. Then she pops her head back into the dining room and says, "And I'm not eating any of those eggs either, Mother. Have you heard of salmonella?" Lynetta walks away and my mother asks, "Salmonella?" She looks at my father. "Could they have salmonella?" "I don't know, Patsy. I'm more worried that our son is scared." "Scared! Rick, please. Bryce is not scared. He's a great kid who's — " "Who's scared of a girl." "Dad, I'm not scared of her, she just annoys me!" "Why?" "You know why! She annoys you too. She's too much about everything!" "Bryce, I wanted you to face your fear, but you just avoided it. If you loved her, that would be different. Love is scary, but this is just embarrassing. She talks a lot, she's too excited about everything, so what? Just talk to her, ask your question, and leave. Stand up to her, please!" "Rick...," my mom tries to calm him, "Rick, he did find out what you asked him to—" "No, he didn't!" "What do you mean?" "He tells me they're all chickens! Of course they're all chickens! The real question is how many are hens and how many are roosters." I suddenly realized my mistake, and I felt really stupid. No wonder he was upset with me. I was such an idiot! They were all chickens... obviously! Garrett acted like he knew everything about chickens, but he knew nothing! Why did I listen to him?

It was already too late. My dad thought I was scared, and to help me get over it, he wanted me to return the carton of eggs to the Bakers and tell them we didn't eat eggs or were allergic to them, or something like that. Then my mom interrupted and said, 'What are you teaching him, Rick? That's not true. If he returns them, shouldn't he tell the truth?' 'What, that you're scared of salmonella poisoning?' 'Me? Aren't you worried, too?' 'Patsy, that's not the point. I don't want a scared son!' 'But teaching him to lie?' 'Fine. Then just throw them away. But from now on, I want you to look that little tiger in the eye, understand?' 'Yes, sir.' 'Okay, then.' I was free from trouble for about eight days. Then she appeared again early in the morning, jumping around on our porch with eggs in her hands. 'Hi, Bryce! Here you go.' I tried to look her in the eye and say no thanks, but she was so happy, and I was too sleepy to handle it. She ended up giving me another carton, and I threw it away in the kitchen trash before my dad came to breakfast. This continued for two years. It became a part of my morning routine. I would watch for Juli so I could open the door before she could knock or ring the bell, and then I'd quickly hide the eggs in the trash before my dad came. Then one day I messed up. Juli had been away because they had cut down the sycamore tree, but one morning she was back, giving eggs. I took them as usual and went to throw them away as usual. But the kitchen trash was so full there was no space for the carton, so I put it on top, grabbed the trash, and rushed out the front door to throw everything in the garbage can outside.

Guess who was standing still on my porch? The Egg Chick. I almost threw the trash everywhere. 'What are you still doing here?' I asked her. 'I...I don't know. I was just... thinking.' 'About what?' I really needed to change the subject. I needed to get past her with the trash before she saw what was on top. She looked away, looking shy. Juli Baker feeling shy? I didn't think that could happen. Anyway, I saw a chance to hide a wet magazine over the egg box, and I did it quickly. Then I tried to rush to the trash can, but she blocked me like she was playing soccer. She stopped me again and asked, 'What happened? Did they break?' Perfect idea. 'Yes, Juli,' I said. 'And I'm really sorry about that.' But I was really praying, 'Please, let me get to the trash can.' It seemed like God didn't hear me. Juli grabbed the trash and found her egg carton. She saw right away that they were not broken. They weren't even cracked. She stood there holding the eggs while I threw away the rest of the trash. 'Why did you throw them out?' she asked in a quiet, shaky voice. So I told her we were worried about salmonella poisoning because her yard was messy and we didn't want to upset her. I said it like we were right and she was wrong, but I felt bad. Really bad. Then she told me that some neighbors had been buying eggs from her. And while I was shocked by this news, she quickly said, 'Do you know I've lost over a hundred dollars giving these eggs to you?' Then she ran across the street crying.

Even though I told myself I didn't ask her for the eggs—that I didn't say we wanted, needed, or liked them—the truth is, I had never seen Juli cry before. She didn't cry when she broke her arm in P.E., when kids teased her at school, or when her brothers left her out. She didn’t even cry when they cut down the sycamore tree. I think she cried then, but I didn't see it. To me, Juli Baker was always too strong to cry. I went to my room to get ready for school, feeling very bad about myself. I had been secretly throwing away eggs for more than two years, avoiding her and my dad. What kind of person did that make me? Why didn't I just say, 'No thanks, I don’t want them, don’t need them, don’t like them. Give them to the snake, maybe?' Was I scared of hurting her feelings? Or was I scared of her?

After the sycamore tree was cut down, everything else started to go wrong. Champ, our dog, passed away. Then, I learned something shocking about the eggs. It was Champ's time to leave us, and although I miss him, I found it harder to accept the truth about the eggs than his death. I still can't believe what I learned about the eggs. In our story, the eggs appeared before the chickens, but the dog was there before all of them. One evening, when I was six, my dad brought home a dog he found. The dog was in his truck, tied down. The dog had been hit by a car at a crossroad, and my dad stopped to help. The dog was very thin and had no collar. My dad said the dog was starving and confused. He wondered how someone could abandon their dog. My whole family was excited on the porch. I was thrilled. A dog! A wonderful, happy dog! I now realize Champ wasn't very pretty, but to a six-year-old, any dog is amazing and lovable. My brothers also thought he looked good, but my mom didn't seem happy. She looked worried and said, 'We can't keep that dog in our house.' My dad replied, 'It's not about owning the dog. It's about being kind.' 'So, you're not giving it to us as a pet?' my mom asked. 'No, that's not what I'm planning,' my dad said. 'What will you do then?' she asked. 'Feed him, bathe him, and maybe we'll find him a new home,' my dad answered. My mom made it clear there was no 'maybe' about finding the dog a new home. My brothers asked, 'We can't keep him?' 'That's right,' she confirmed. 'But Mom,' they complained.

"We can't talk about this," she said. "He needs a bath, food, and an ad in the newspaper." My dad hugged Matt and Mike. "One day, boys, we'll get a puppy." My mom was walking back inside, but she said, "Not until you boys clean your room!" By the end of the week, the dog was named Champ. By the next week, he was in the kitchen. Soon, he lived with us completely. It seemed no one else wanted an adult dog that barked happily. Only most of our family did. Then my mom noticed a strange smell. We all smelled it, but didn't know where it was from. My mom thought it was Champ, but we didn't agree. We washed him so much, he smelled like roses. I thought maybe Matt and Mike weren't washing enough, but I didn't check. Since we couldn't agree on the source, we called it the Mystery Smell. We talked about it a lot at dinner, which was funny to my brothers but not to my mom. Then one day, my mom figured it out. She was very angry and thought it was Champ. Dad had to quickly take Champ outside. Mom was upset. "I knew it was him. The Mystery Smell is from the dog peeing! Did you see? He peed on the furniture!" Dad hurried to clean it with paper towels and asked, "Where? Where is it?" There were just a few drops on the table leg. "There," my mom pointed, still upset. "There!"

Dad cleaned it up, then looked at the carpet and said, 'It's just a tiny spill.' 'Exactly!' my mom replied, hands on her hips. 'That's why I can never find anything. The dog has to stay outside from now on. Do you understand? He can't come inside the house anymore!' 'What about the garage?' I asked. 'Can he sleep there?' 'And let him mark everything there? No way!' Mike and Matt were smiling at each other. 'Mystery Pisser! That's a cool name for our band!' 'Yeah! Awesome!' 'Band?' my mom questioned. 'Hold on, what band?' But they had already run to their room, laughing about what their band's logo could be. Dad and I spent the day trying to find and clean up messes. Dad used a bottle of ammonia; I used Lysol after him. We tried to get my brothers to help, but they ended up having a fight with the spray bottles, which got them sent to their room, and they didn't mind. So Champ had to live outside, and he would have been our only pet if not for my fifth-grade science fair. Everyone else had great ideas for their projects, but I couldn't think of one. Then Mrs. Brubeck, our teacher, pulled me aside and mentioned her friend had chickens and could give me a fertilized egg for my project. 'But I don't know how to hatch an egg,' I said. She smiled and comforted me. 'You don't need to know everything right away, Juli. The point is to learn something new.' 'But what if it dies?' I asked. 'If it dies, it dies. Just record your experiment scientifically and you'll still get an A, if you're worried about that.' An A? I was more worried about being responsible for a chick's death. Suddenly, making a volcano, creating neoprene, or showing how gears work seemed much more appealing.

The ball was already rolling, and Mrs. Brubeck didn't want to talk anymore. She took out a book called The Beginner's Guide to Raising Chickens and told me, 'Read about how to make eggs hatch using machines and prepare tonight. I'll bring you an egg tomorrow.' 'But ...' 'Don't worry, Juli,' she said. 'We do this every year and it's always a great project at the fair.' I tried to say 'But...' again, but she had already left to help another student make up their mind. That night, I was very worried. I read the chapter about hatching eggs four times and still didn't know how to start. I didn't have an old fish tank! We didn't have a thermometer for hatching eggs! Could I use a cooking thermometer instead? I also needed to manage the moisture in the air, or the baby chick would be in trouble. If it was too dry, the chick couldn't break out of the egg; if it was too wet, it could die from being too soft. Mushy chick disease?! My mom, who is very practical, told me to tell Mrs. Brubeck that I couldn't hatch a chick. 'Why not grow beans instead?' she suggested. My dad, on the other hand, knew I had to do my teacher's assignment and said he would help. 'Making an incubator isn't hard. We'll build one after dinner.' My dad always knows where to find things in our garage, which amazes me. While he was making a hole in a piece of old plastic, he told me he had hatched a duck when he was in high school. 'A duck?' I asked. 'Yes, but it works the same way for all birds. Keep the temperature steady and the moisture right, turn the egg a few times a day, and in a few weeks, you'll have a little bird.'

He gave me a lightbulb and a long wire with a plug. 'Put this through the hole in the clear plastic. I'll look for some thermometers.' 'More than one?' I asked. 'Yes, we need to make a device to measure moisture,' he explained. 'A moisture meter?' I asked. 'Yes, it's like a thermometer wrapped in damp cloth,' he said. I smiled. 'So no sick chicks?' He smiled too. 'Exactly.' The next day, I was keeping six chicken eggs warm at 102 degrees Fahrenheit. Mrs. Brubeck warned me, 'Not all will survive, Juli. Hope for one, but three is the best we've seen. Your grade depends on your notes. Be a scientist. Good luck.' Then she left. Notes? About what? I only had to turn the eggs three times a day and watch the warmth and moisture. What else was there to do? That evening, my dad came to the garage with a cardboard tube and a flashlight. He attached them so the light shone straight through the tube. 'I'll show you how to check an egg,' he said, then turned off the garage light. I remembered seeing something about checking eggs in Mrs. Brubeck's book, but I hadn't read it yet. 'Why do they call it that?' I asked him. 'And why do we do it?' 'People used candles before we had light bulbs,' he explained. He held an egg up to the tube. 'The light helps you see inside the egg to check the baby chick. Then we can remove any that aren't growing right.' 'Remove them?' I asked. 'Yes, take out the ones that aren't growing well,' he explained. 'But doesn't that kill them?' I asked. He looked at me. 'Leaving a bad egg might ruin the good ones.' 'Why? Wouldn't it just not hatch?' I asked. He continued checking the egg. 'It might break and make the other eggs sick.'

This project was becoming very difficult due to sick chicks, eggs that could explode, and having to remove unhealthy chicks. Then my father showed me something amazing. He said, 'Look here, Julianna. You can see the embryo.' He used a flashlight to help me see inside an egg. I looked and saw a dark spot surrounded by veins, which looked like a bean. Suddenly, I realized the egg was alive. I checked the other eggs and they all had little embryos that looked like beans too! I hoped they would all survive. I asked my dad if we could move the incubator inside because it might be too cold outside at night. He agreed and suggested we keep the door open while moving it. For the next two weeks, I was very busy with the eggs. I marked them with letters A to F, and soon gave them names: Abby, Bonnie, Clyde, Dexter, Eunice, and Florence. Every day I weighed them, checked them with a light, and turned them. I even tried making chicken sounds to help them, but it was too tiring, so I hummed to them instead. I was always humming when I was near my eggs because it made me happy. I read a book about raising chickens many times. For my project, I made drawings of how a chick develops, a big poster of a chicken, and charts about the egg temperatures, humidity, and weight loss. Eggs might seem boring from the outside, but I knew exciting things were happening inside! Two days before the science fair, I was checking an egg named Bonnie and saw something incredible. I thought I saw the heart beating! I called my dad to look, and after he checked, he went to get my mom.

We all gathered around and watched Bonnie's heart beat. Even my mom agreed it was really cool. Clyde started to hatch just as I was about to go to school. His beak broke through the egg a little, and then he took a rest. He tried again, but then rested more. I didn't want to go to school and leave him. What if he needed me? I thought maybe I should stay home for a bit! My dad said that hatching takes a long time and I could see more after school. But I wanted to see all the chicks hatch, not miss a moment. I said, 'I can't miss any of the hatching!' My mom suggested, 'Why don't you take it to school?' She said my teacher, Mrs. Brubeck, would be okay with it because it was her idea. Having a smart mom helps. I decided to set up my science fair project early. I took everything—posters and charts—to school with my mom's help. Mrs. Brubeck was fine with it. She was helping other kids, so I watched the chicks hatch all day at school. Clyde and Bonnie hatched first. They looked tired and messy at first. But when Abby and Dexter hatched, Bonnie and Clyde started to look better and ready for action.

The last two eggs took a long time to hatch, but Mrs. Brubeck told me to leave them alone, and they hatched during the fair that night. My whole family was there, but Matt and Mike only watched for a little while before they went to see something else. My mom and dad stayed the whole time. Mom even held Bonnie and cuddled her. Later that night, when everything was done and I was getting ready to go home, Mom asked if the chicks were going back to Mrs. Brubeck. I was confused and asked, 'What chicks?' Mom said, 'The chicks, Juli. You're not going to keep them, are you?' I hadn't thought about what to do after they hatched. I had just focused on hatching them. But Mom was right, they were here now. There were six cute fluffy chicks, each with its own name and personality. I didn't know what to do, so I said I would ask Mrs. Brubeck. I found Mrs. Brubeck and hoped she wouldn't want them back. I had hatched and named them. I had even saved them from a disease. They were mine! Luckily, Mrs. Brubeck agreed they were mine. 'Have fun,' she said and left to help Heidi with her project. Mom was silent all the way home. She didn't want chickens as much as she wanted a tractor or a goat. I quietly asked, 'Please, Mom?' as we stopped at home. She covered her face and asked, 'Where will we raise chickens, Juli?' I suggested, 'In the backyard?' I didn't know what else to say. 'What about Champ?' Mom asked. 'They'll get along. I'll teach him. I promise.'

My dad whispered, 'They can take care of themselves, Trina.' But then my brothers shouted, 'Champ will scare them away, Mom,' and they kept joking. 'Yes! But you won't see it because they are already yellow!' 'Whoa! Yellow Already—cool name.' 'That might work! But wait—people might think it's about our stomachs!' 'Oh, right—never mind!' 'Yes, let him scare the chicks.' My brothers looked at each other, excited, and started joking again. 'Scare the Chicks! You get it?' 'You mean like we are chick scarers? Or we scare the chicks?' Dad turned and said, 'Out. Both of you, leave. Find a name somewhere else.' So they rushed out, and we three sat in the car, only hearing my little birds chirping quietly. Then my mom sighed deeply and said, 'They don't cost much to care for, do they?' My dad nodded. 'They eat bugs, Trina. And a bit of food. They don't need much care.' 'Bugs? Really? What kind of bugs?' 'Earwigs, worms, pill bugs... maybe spiders, if they can catch them. I think they eat snails, too.' 'Really?' My mom smiled. 'Well, then...' 'Oh, thank you, Mom. Thank you!' That's how we ended up having chickens. We didn't realize that six chickens looking for bugs would also ruin the grass. In six months, our yard was destroyed. We also didn't think that chicken food would attract mice, and mice would attract wild cats. Champ was good at keeping the cats away, but they waited around the front or side yard, just waiting for him to rest so they could sneak in. Then my brothers started catching the mice, which I thought was to help. I didn't know until I heard my mom screaming from their room. They were actually raising a big snake.

Mom was very upset and it seemed like she might kick us out. But then, I found out something amazing—chickens lay eggs! They were beautiful, shiny, and creamy white. I found one under Bonnie, another under Clyde (who I then called Clydette), and one more in Florence's bed. I was so excited about the eggs! I showed them to my mom.
