She was shocked at first and looked very tired. She thought they were baby chicks and got worried. But I told her they were just eggs. Mom looked relieved when I reminded her that we don't have a rooster. She laughed and seemed to feel better. She asked me how many eggs the chickens might lay, but I didn't know. It turned out our chickens laid so many eggs that we couldn't eat them all. We tried boiling and pickling them, but it was too much, and mom said it was getting expensive. One day, while I was collecting eggs, our neighbor Mrs. Stueby asked if she could buy any extra eggs from us. She offered two dollars for a dozen eggs. I agreed happily. She told me to sell to her first before selling to another neighbor, Mrs. Helms. Thanks to Mrs. Stueby and Mrs. Helms, we didn't have too many eggs anymore. I thought about giving the money to my mom since the chickens had messed up the backyard, but she told me to keep it. So, I saved the money for myself.

One day, while I was walking to Mrs. Helms' house, Mrs. Loski drove past. She waved and smiled at me, and I felt guilty because I thought she believed I was giving away eggs for free, out of kindness. But actually, Mrs. Helms and Mrs. Stueby were paying me for the eggs. I liked earning money from selling eggs because I didn't usually get regular pocket money at home. However, I started to think that I should give some eggs to Mrs. Loski for free because she had been very helpful to us. She would lend us things and even be late to work to help my mom. Giving her some eggs seemed like a small thing to do in return. Also, I liked seeing Bryce, Mrs. Loski's son, when I delivered the eggs. Bryce seemed different and nicer than he was at school. After a few times of giving eggs to the Loskis, Bryce started to expect me and would thank me. Even though Mrs. Helms and Mrs. Stueby offered me more money, seeing Bryce made it worth it. So, I kept delivering eggs to them throughout middle school. The best eggs always went to the Loskis, and I enjoyed the moments I got to spend with Bryce.

They cut down the sycamore tree. Two weeks later, Champ, our dog, died. He had been sleeping a lot. We did not know how old he was, but it was not a shock when Dad found him dead one night. We buried him in the backyard and my brothers made a cross for his grave that says: HERE LIES THE MYSTERY PISSER P.I.P. I felt very sad and confused for some time. It was raining a lot, so I rode my bike to school instead of taking the bus. When I got home, I would go to my room, read a book, and forget about collecting eggs. Mrs. Stueby helped me get back on track. She called because she read about the tree in the newspaper and felt bad for us. She also missed getting eggs from us and was worried the chickens would stop laying eggs because they were stressed. She told me, 'Stress can make chickens lose their feathers and stop laying eggs. I'm allergic to feathers, or I would have chickens too. Just bring me eggs when you can. I just wanted to check on you and say I'm sorry about your tree and your dog. Your mom told me he died.' So, I started collecting and cleaning the eggs again. One morning, I had enough eggs and delivered them. I went to Mrs. Stueby's house, then Mrs. Helms', and finally to the Loskis'. When I got to the Loskis', I realized I hadn't seen Bryce in a long time. We were both at school, but I was too busy to notice him. My heart beat faster when the door opened and Bryce looked at me with his blue eyes. I barely managed to say, 'Here.' He took the half-carton of eggs and said, 'You know, you don't have to give us these….'

"I understand," I replied, looking down. We stood silently for a very long time. Then he asked, "Will you start taking the bus again?" I looked at him, unsure, and said, "I'm not sure. I haven't gone there since... you know." He mentioned, "It doesn't look bad now. It's clear. They might begin building soon." To me, that sounded terrible. "Well," he continued, "I need to prepare for school. See you there." He smiled and shut the door. I felt strange and disconnected, just standing there. Would I ever return to Collier Street? My mom said I had to. Was I making it more difficult for myself? Suddenly, the door opened quickly and Bryce came out, holding a very full trash can. "Juli!" he exclaimed. "Why are you still here?" I was surprised and confused about why I was still there. I might have run home, but Bryce was struggling with the trash, trying to push it down. I offered to help, noticing he might spill it. That's when I saw part of an egg carton. It wasn't just any carton; it was the one I had given him. I could see eggs inside. I asked him, "What happened? Did you drop them?" He quickly replied, "Yes, and I'm really sorry." He tried to stop me, but I took the carton out of the trash and asked, "All of them?" I opened it and was shocked to see six perfect eggs. "Why did you throw these out?" He walked past me to the trash bin, and I followed him, waiting for an explanation. He emptied the trash and faced me, asking, "Do you know what salmonella is?" "Salmonella? But..."
I walked home feeling embarrassed and confused, my heart completely broken.

It didn't take long for me to realize that I'd traded in my old problems with Juli Baker for a whole new set of problems with Juli Baker. I could feel her anger from far away. It was actually worse when she was angry at me than when she was bothering me. Why? Because I made a mistake, that's why. I was very embarrassed, and trying to blame her yard didn't help at all. The way she ignored me, or clearly avoided me, loudly reminded me that I had been mean. A very mean jerk.

Then one day, after school, I was coming home from hanging out with my friend Garrett, and I saw Juli in her front yard, cutting a shrub aggressively. She was really going at it. Branches were flying everywhere, and even from across the street, I could hear her making noises and saying things like, 'No...you... don't! You are coming... off... whether you like it or... not!' Did I feel good about this? No, I did not. Yes, their yard was messy, and it was good that someone was cleaning it, but why was it Juli doing it? Where was her dad? What about her brothers, Matt and Mike? Why Juli?
Because I had embarrassed her, that's why. I felt worse than ever.
So I went inside and tried to ignore the fact that from my desk and my window, I could see Juli across the street, attacking a bush. It was hard to focus. I didn't get any homework done.
The next day at school, I wanted to talk to her, but I never got the chance. She wouldn't let me come close to her.

On the way home, I thought about something. It scared me a bit at first, but then I thought it was a good idea to help her in the garden to make up for being mean. I hoped she wouldn't be too bossy or act silly. I planned to tell her I was sorry for being mean and wanted to help her trim the bushes. That was it. If she was still upset, that was her issue. But I never got to do it. When I walked from the bus stop, I saw my grandfather was already doing it. I was shocked because my grandfather never did garden work. He always wore slippers, not work boots and clothes for yard work. I hid and watched them for a while, getting angrier because he was talking to her more than he ever talked to me. I sneaked home by a different way to avoid them. I didn't do my homework because I was upset watching them. Juli was having a good time with my grandfather, who I had never seen really happy before. But there he was, laughing and working in the garden. At dinner, he looked different, like he was happier and more alive.

"Good evening," he said as he sat down with us. "Oh, Patsy, that looks tasty!" "Well, Dad," my mom laughed, "your short walk across the street really helped you." "Yes," my father said. "Patsy said you were there all afternoon. If you wanted to fix things at home, why didn't you tell us?" My father was joking, but I think my grandfather was a bit upset. He took a cheese-stuffed potato and said, "Pass the salt, please, Bryce." There was some tension between my father and grandfather, but it could have gone away if Dad had stopped talking about it. But Dad continued, "So why is the girl the one fixing up their place?" My grandfather took his time salting his potato, then looked at me. Uh-oh, I thought. Uh-oh. I suddenly realized that my secret about throwing away eggs wasn't safe. For two years, I had been hiding how I felt about Juli, her eggs, her chickens, and her visits. Granddad knew, I saw it in his eyes. He was about to reveal everything, and I was in trouble. Suddenly, my grandfather just looked at my father and said, "She just wants to." I was sweating a lot, and as my father said, "Well, it's good someone finally did," my grandfather gave me a look that said he wouldn't forget this. We had another serious talk, and this time, I couldn't just walk away. After we finished eating, I went to my room, but my grandfather followed, closed the door, and sat on my bed quietly. He didn't make any noise at all. He moved through my room like a ghost.

I kept hitting my knee and dropping my pencil, feeling really awkward. But I tried to act cool and said, 'Hello, Granddad. Are you here to see my room?' He just stared at me without saying anything. I broke down and said, 'Look, Granddad, I know I made a mistake. I should have told her the truth, but I just couldn't. I thought the problem would go away. I mean, how long do chickens keep laying eggs? They started back in fifth grade! That was three years ago! Don't they stop at some point? And what could I do? Tell her my mom was scared of getting sick from the eggs? And my dad wanted me to say we were allergic—no one would believe that. So I kept throwing them away. I didn't realize she could have sold them. I thought they were just extra.' He listened but didn't respond quickly. I sighed and thanked him for not bringing it up during dinner. I felt I owed him for that. He looked out the window and said, 'Your character forms when you're young. The choices you make now will impact your whole life.' He was silent for a bit, then said, 'I hate to see you get in too deep to get out.' I replied, 'Yes, sir.' He looked serious and said, 'Don't just agree with me, Bryce.' Then he stood up and said, 'Think about what I've told you, and next time, make the right choice. It's better for everyone in the end.' Then suddenly, he left. The next day, I played basketball at Garrett's. When Garrett's mom dropped me off, my granddad didn't even notice. He was busy doing something in Juli's yard. I tried to do my homework, but my mom got home and started talking a lot. Then Lynetta came and she and my mom argued about whether Lynetta's makeup made her look bad. Lynetta never seems to learn.

I gathered my things and went to my room to escape, but it was useless. There was a loud saw noise from across the street and I could hear the loud banging of a hammer in between the saw's noises. When I looked out the window, I saw Juli working hard. She was holding nails in her mouth and hitting them with a hammer like it was easy. For a moment, I imagined she was hitting my head with the hammer, and I got scared. I quickly closed the curtain, forgot about my homework, and decided to watch TV instead. All week, people were busy fixing things around the house. Every evening, my granddad would come home happy and hungry, always praising my mom's cooking. Then came Saturday, and the last thing I wanted was to stay home while my granddad and Juli worked on her yard. My mom wanted me to work on our yard, but it felt silly compared to what was happening across the street. So, I stayed in my room and tried to call my friend Garrett, but he wasn't home. I tried others, but everyone was busy. Asking my parents for a ride to the movies or the mall was no use; they wanted me to work on the yard. I felt trapped. I ended up just watching Juli and my granddad from my window, which was really boring. My granddad caught me watching and told Juli, which made me feel embarrassed. I quickly left the house by the back door and jumped over the fence. I just had to get away.

I think I walked ten miles that day. I was really upset, but I wasn't sure if I was more upset with my grandfather, Juli, or myself. What was wrong with me? If I wanted to make things right with Juli, why didn't I just go over and help her? What was holding me back? I ended up at my friend Garrett's house, and I was so happy to see him. Garrett is great at making you forget your troubles. We played basketball, watched TV, and talked about going to the water slides in the summer. When I got home, I saw Juli watering the garden. She saw me, but she didn't wave or smile. She just looked away. Usually, I would pretend not to see her or just wave quickly and go inside. But she had been mad at me for a long time. She hadn't spoken to me since the incident with the eggs. A few days earlier in math class, I smiled at her to say sorry, but she ignored me and looked away. I tried to talk to her outside the classroom about the garden and how sorry I felt, but she avoided me. So there she was, watering the garden, and I felt really bad. I walked up to her and told her the garden looked great and that she did a nice job. She just said 'Thanks' without smiling and mentioned that Chet did most of it. Chet? I was surprised she called my grandfather by his first name. I tried to apologize for what I had done, but she just kept looking at the water on the ground.

Finally she asked, 'I don't understand, Bryce. Why didn't you just tell me?' 'I...I don't know. It was silly. I should have told you. And I shouldn't have mentioned the yard. That was wrong.' I was already feeling a lot better. Then Juli said, 'Maybe this is good,' and she started to jump lightly, acting like her usual self. 'Doesn't it look great? I learned so much from Chet. You're so lucky. I don't have grandparents anymore.' 'Oh,' I replied, unsure what to say next. 'I do feel bad for him, though. He really misses your grandma.' Then she laughed and said, 'Can you believe it? He thinks I remind him of her.' 'What?' 'Yes,' she laughed again. 'That's what I thought. But he meant it nicely.' I looked at Juli and tried to imagine my grandma when she was young. It was impossible. Juli has long, fluffy brown hair and a face with lots of freckles, while my grandma was always blonde. And my grandma used a lot of white powder on her face and hair, even in her shoes and on her body. She used powder on everything. I couldn't imagine Juli covered in white powder. Maybe gun powder, but not the scented white kind. Forget it. I think I was staring, because Juli said, 'Look, I didn't say it, he did. I just thought it was nice.' 'Yeah, okay. Well, good luck with the lawn. I'm sure it will grow well.' Then I surprised myself by saying, 'Knowing you, you'll make it work.' I didn't mean it in a bad way, I really meant it. I laughed, and then she laughed, and that's how I left her—watering the new grass, smiling. I hadn't felt this happy in weeks. The problems with the eggs were finally over. I felt free and happy. It took me a few minutes at dinner to notice that I was the only happy one.

Lynetta was making a sad face, which she often did. My dad greeted me by immediately complaining about the grass. 'No problem,' I replied. 'I'll handle it tomorrow.' But my dad just frowned at me. Then, my mom asked my granddad if he was tired today. I didn't even see him sitting there quietly. 'Yes,' my dad said loudly across the table. 'Is that girl making you work too hard?' My granddad neatly placed his fork on his napkin and replied, 'That girl's name is Juli, and no, she is not making me work too hard.' 'Insensitive, am I?' laughed my dad. 'You really like that girl, don't you?' Even Lynetta stopped frowning for a moment because these were harsh words. My mom tried to calm Dad by kicking him gently, but it only made him angrier. 'No, Patsy! I want to know why your dad likes a stranger so much when he never even played catch with his own grandson!' I agreed, but then I remembered I was very grateful to my granddad. Without thinking, I said, 'Calm down, Dad. Juli reminds him of Grandma.' Everyone went silent and looked at me. So I turned to my granddad and said, 'Right, Granddad?' He nodded and fiddled with his fork again. 'Of Renée?' My dad asked, looking from my mom to my granddad. 'She can't remind you of her!' My granddad closed his eyes and said, 'It's her spirit that reminds me of Renée.' 'Her spirit,' my dad repeated skeptically. 'Yes, her spirit,' my granddad said quietly, then asked, 'Do you know why the Bakers haven't cleaned up their yard? 'Why? Of course. They're messy, that's why. They have a rundown house, two old cars, and a messy yard.' 'They are not messy, Rick. They are good, honest, hardworking people,' my granddad defended, 'who just don't care much about appearances.'

We have lived across the street from those people for more than six years, and there's no excuse for how they live. 'No?' My grandfather breathes deeply and thinks for a bit. Then he asks, 'Rick, if you had a brother or sister or child who was very sick or couldn't move well, what would you do?' It shocked everyone like a loud noise in a quiet church. My dad looked upset, shook his head, and said, 'Chet, what does that have to do with anything?' My grandfather stares at him, then quietly mentions, 'Juli's dad has a brother who needs a lot of help.' My dad laughs and interrupts, 'That makes sense now!' 'Makes sense?' my grandfather repeats calmly. 'Yes, it explains why those people are the way they are,' my dad smiles at us. 'It must be something in their family,' he adds. Everyone is shocked. Lynetta can't speak, and my mom scolds, 'Rick!' but my dad just laughs nervously and jokes, 'It was just a joke! Clearly, something's wrong with them. Oh, sorry Chet. I forgot, the girl reminds you of Renée.' 'Rick!' my mom scolds him again, this time angrier. 'Oh, Patsy, please. Your dad is just being too dramatic, making me feel bad for talking about our neighbors because they have a family issue. Other people have problems too but still take care of their yard. They should care more about their house,' my dad says. My grandfather's face turns red, but he calmly explains, 'They don't own the house, Rick. It's the landlord's job to take care of it, but he doesn't. And since Juli's dad looks after his brother, all their money goes to his care, which is very expensive.'

Very quietly my mom asks, 'Don't they have places run by the government for this?' 'I'm not sure, Patsy. Maybe there are no government places nearby. Maybe they thought a private place was better for him.' 'Still,' my dad says, 'there are government places available, and if they choose not to use them, that's their decision. It's not our fault their family had a genetic issue, and I won't feel bad for wanting — ' My grandfather slams his hand on the table and half-stands, saying, 'It's not about genes, Rick! It happened because he didn't get enough oxygen when he was born.' He lowers his voice, but his words sound even stronger. 'Juli's uncle had the umbilical cord around his neck. Twice. One minute he was a healthy baby, just like your son, Bryce, and the next he was permanently hurt.' My mother suddenly became very upset. In moments, she was crying loudly, and my father was trying to soothe her. It didn't help. She just broke down right there. Lynetta threw her napkin down and muttered, 'This family is a joke,' and left quickly. Then my mother ran out of the room, crying, and my father followed her quickly, giving my grandfather a very angry look. That left Granddad and me at a table with cold food. 'Wow,' I finally said. 'I had no idea.' 'You still don't,' he replied. 'What do you mean?' I asked. He sat still for a moment, then leaned toward me and asked, 'Why do you think your mother is so upset?' 'I...I don't know.' I tried to smile and said, 'Because she's a woman?' He smiled a little and said, 'No. She's upset because she knows she could be in Mr. Baker's situation.' I thought for a moment and then asked, 'Did her brother have the cord around his neck when he was born?' He shook his head.

He leaned in closer and whispered, 'You did.' 'I did?' He nodded. 'Twice.' 'But...' 'The doctor who helped you be born was very alert. Also, there was some extra room in the cord, so he managed to fix it as you were born. You were safe when you were born, but it could have been dangerous.' If someone had told me long ago that I was born with the cord around my neck, I would have joked about it or ignored the topic. But after recent events, I was very scared and couldn't stop thinking about what if things had been different? What would have happened to me? It seemed like my dad wouldn't care much for me. He might have sent me away and forgotten about me. But then I thought, no, he's my dad, he wouldn't do that...would he? I looked around at our big house, the white carpet, the old and beautiful things everywhere. Would they have given up all these things to make me happy? I didn't think so. I would have been an embarrassment to them. Appearances were very important to my parents, especially my dad. My granddad spoke softly, 'You can't think about what could have been, Bryce.' Then, as if he knew what I was thinking, he said, 'And it's not right to blame him for something he didn't do.' I nodded and tried to calm down, but it was hard. Then he said, 'By the way, I liked what you said earlier.' 'What?' I asked, my throat tight. 'About your grandmother. How did you know that?' I shook my head and said, 'Juli told me.' 'Oh? You spoke with her, then?' 'Yes. I actually apologized to her.'

Well...! And I was feeling much better about everything, but now... God, I feel so silly again. 'Don't worry about it. You said sorry, and that's important,' he said as he stood up. 'Hey, I feel like going for a walk. Do you want to come with me?' I really wanted to go to my room, lock the door, and be alone. 'Walking really helps clear my head,' he mentioned, and that's when I understood this was not just a walk, but a chance to spend time together. I stood up and agreed, 'Yes. Let's go.' Although my granddad usually didn't talk much to me, just asking for the salt, he talked a lot this time. We walked around our area and beyond, and I learned that my granddad knows many things and is also quite funny in a quiet, clever way. His words and the way he spoke were really interesting. As we returned to our area, we passed a new house being built where an old tree once stood. My granddad paused, looked at the sky, and remarked, 'There must have been a great view from here.' I looked up and noticed the stars for the first time that night. 'Did you ever see her up there?' I asked him. 'Your mom showed her to me once as we passed by. It was frightening to see her so high, but I understood why after reading the article,' he explained, shaking his head. 'The tree is gone, but the inspiration it gave her remains. Understand?' Fortunately, I didn't have to respond. He just smiled and said, 'People are different, some plain, some shiny, some very shiny... But sometimes, you meet someone who shines very brightly, and they are unforgettable.' As we got to our front porch, my grandfather put his arm around me and said, 'I really enjoyed our walk, Bryce.' 'Me too,' I replied, and we went inside.

As soon as we arrived, it felt like a battlefield. Although nobody was shouting or crying, I could see from my parents' expressions that something bad had happened while my granddad and I were away. Granddad quietly said to me, 'I need to fix another problem,' and went to speak to my parents in the dining room. I didn't want to be part of that mood. I went to my room, shut the door, and threw myself onto my bed in the dark. I lay there for a while, thinking about the ruined dinner. When I couldn't think about it anymore, I sat up and looked out the window. I saw a light in the Bakers' house and the streetlights were on, but the night felt extra dark and heavy. I tried to look at the stars from my window, but I couldn't see them. I wondered if Juli had ever climbed the sycamore tree at night to be closer to the stars. I thought about how Juli always seemed uninteresting to me. I turned on my desk lamp and found the newspaper article about Juli that I had thrown in a drawer. The article praised Juli a lot, saying she was trying to protect our town from too much building. They called her a 'strong voice' and a 'radiant beacon.' I didn't agree with them. I think if someone wants to cut down a tree on their land to build a house, they should be able to. That's their choice. The article was too praising for my taste. But the parts where Juli spoke were different. Unlike the rest of the article, Juli's words seemed thoughtful and serious. To her, sitting in that tree meant something deep.

It all made sense to me. She described feeling like she was above everything when sitting in the tree, and how special that felt. 'Being above the ground and touched by the wind,' she explained, 'feels like beauty has touched your heart.' Who else in junior high could say something like that? Definitely not my friends. There were other interesting points too, like how some things are greater than the sum of their parts, and why people need things that make their lives feel extraordinary and full of wonder. I kept reading her words over and over, amazed at how she thought like that. Juli Baker is definitely smart, but this was more than just being good at school. If I had read this article a month ago, I would have thrown it away, calling it nonsense, but now it clicked with me. A month ago, I wouldn't have cared about Juli's photo either, but now I couldn't stop looking at it. Not the photo showing the whole scene, but the one closer up of her in the tree, looking into the distance with the wind in her hair, like a captain sailing into the sunset. I had ignored Juli Baker for so many years, but now I found myself unable to look away. I felt something strange in my stomach, and it wasn't a good feeling; it actually frightened me. I hid the newspaper under my pillow, trying to forget how annoying Juli Baker was. But soon, I was thinking about her again and took the paper back out. This was crazy! What was I doing?
I forced myself to turn off the light and go to sleep. I was losing control, and it was really time to take charge.

I had never felt ashamed of our home before. I had never looked at our house or our street and wished we lived in the nicer, newer houses nearby. This was where I grew up. This was my home. I knew our yard wasn't perfect. My mom often complained about it quietly, but I didn't think it was a big issue. Or at least I thought so. Maybe I should have paid more attention. Why did we ignore the outside but keep the inside so clean? Our house was very clean, except for my brothers' room. After finding a snake there, my mom stopped cleaning their room. She said if they were old enough to have a snake, they should clean their room themselves. Matt and Mike just kept their door closed all the time after that. I didn't think much about money or that we didn't have a lot of it. I knew we weren't rich, but I never felt like I was missing out on anything important. My brothers often asked for things, and my mom would say no, explaining we couldn't afford it. I thought she meant they didn't deserve it or need it. But then Bryce called our house a dump, and I started to notice other things. It wasn't just the yard. It was my dad's old truck, my mom's car, our rusty family bike, and the fact that when we bought something new, it was always second-hand. We also never went on vacations. I wondered why, considering how hard my parents worked. My dad was very hardworking, and my mom did office jobs when she could. What was the point of all their hard work if this was all we had?

I thought it was rude to ask my parents if we were poor. But as time passed, I felt I needed to ask them. Every day, I rode our old bike home from school, passed our broken fence and uneven lawn, and planned to ask them that evening. But I always hesitated. Then, one day, I got an idea to fix the yard and talk about our situation. That night, with my brothers out and a quiet dinner table, I suggested fixing the yard with some nails, a hammer, and paint, and asked about the cost of grass seed. My parents stopped eating and looked at me. I mentioned I could handle the tools and make it a project. My mom looked away from me to my dad. My dad told me that the yard was not our responsibility but belonged to Mr. Finnegan. I was shocked and asked, 'Who is Mr. Finnegan?' My dad explained he was our landlord, the owner of the house. I was surprised and asked, 'We don't own the house?' They silently communicated, and then my dad admitted he thought I knew we rented. I questioned why landlords weren't fixing things like leaks and clogs, as my dad always did those jobs. My dad explained it was simpler than asking the landlord for help and it prevented our rent from increasing.

My mother took my hand and said, 'Sweetheart, I'm sorry if this surprises you. We thought you already knew.' 'But why does the yard look bad? Why do we take care of inside the house but not outside?' My father looked unhappy and explained, 'When we agreed to rent this place, the owner promised to fix the fences and put new grass in the front yard. But he never did.' He shook his head. 'Fixing everything would cost a lot, and it doesn't make sense to spend so much on a house we don't own. Plus, it's a matter of principle.' 'But we live here,' I said quietly, 'and it looks really bad.' My father looked at me closely. 'Julianna, what's wrong?' 'Nothing, Daddy,' I answered, but he knew I wasn't telling the truth. 'Sweetheart,' he said softly, 'tell me.' I knew how he would react if I told him, but I couldn't keep it to myself, especially with the way he was looking at me. So, I took a deep breath and told him, 'The Loskis have been throwing away my eggs because they're worried they might have salmonella from our messy yard.' My father said, 'Oh, that's silly,' but my mother was shocked. 'What?' she exclaimed. 'Did Patsy tell you that?' I looked down. 'No, Bryce did.' 'This must have been talked about by the whole family! A boy wouldn't think of that by himself ... !' My mother looked very upset. She covered her face and said, 'I can't keep living like this! Robert, we need to make some changes. We really do!' 'Trina, I'm doing all I can. I'm sorry about the yard and the situation. This isn't how I imagined our life would be either, but sometimes we have to make sacrifices for what's right.'

My mother stopped looking at her hands and said, 'This is not good for our family. Your daughter is unhappy because we don't fix our yard.' 'It's not our yard,' my father replied. 'How can you say that? Robert, wake up! We have been here for twelve years. It's not temporary anymore! If we want a nice place with our own yard, if we want to help the kids with college or do other things we promised, we need to move him to government care.' My father sighed deeply and whispered, 'We've talked about this many times, Trina. You always end up agreeing that keeping him at Greenhaven is the right choice.' I wanted to say, 'Wait! What are you talking about? Who are you talking about?' But they were talking so fast and arguing so much that I couldn't get a word in, and soon it felt like I wasn't even there. Then it hit me. They were talking about my dad's brother, my Uncle David. To me, Uncle David was just a name. My parents told me about him, but I never met him. Even though my dad visited him, he never said when. He never talked about it. Dad also said we shouldn't talk about Uncle David to others because David was mentally disabled. 'People jump to conclusions,' he had said. 'They might think something is wrong with you too. Trust me, I know.' So we never talked about it. Not at home, not with friends. It was like Uncle David didn't exist. But now, he seemed very important, and from their argument, I understood that he was why we didn't have our own house, nice cars, or fancy things. He was why my parents always seemed so tired. Why did I even bring up the yard? I had never seen my parents argue like this.

I really wanted to tell them to stop fighting because they loved each other. But I just sat there crying. My mom suddenly stopped and said we shouldn't fight in front of me. My dad apologized and comforted me, telling me not to cry and that it wasn't my fault. They promised to solve their problems. My mom tried to smile through her tears and said they would always manage. That night, they each came to talk to me separately. My dad talked about his brother whom he loved and promised to always look after. My mom talked about loving my dad for being strong and kind, about dreams and appreciating what we have. She made me cry again when she kissed me goodnight and said I was her greatest joy. I felt sad for both of my parents, but I also felt lucky they were mine. The next morning, as I rode my old bike to school, I decided to clean up our yard when I got back home. I wanted to make our place nicer, even though we only rented it. However, fixing the yard was harder than I thought. I spent a long time looking for tools in the garage. Then, I wasn't sure how to start fixing the yard. I wondered whether to remove or trim the shrubs, and whether to tear down or repair the fence. Maybe I could use some parts of the fence to fix other parts.

The more I looked around, the more I wanted to forget about fixing things. Why should I care? It wasn't even our property. Mr. Finnegan, the owner, should fix it himself. But then I remembered what my mother said last night. I thought to myself that a few bushes and old wood shouldn't stop me from doing something good! So, I grabbed the clippers and started working. After thirty minutes, I learned that cutting one bush makes a lot of branches, and the pile of cut branches gets bigger very fast. It was crazy! I didn't know where to put all these branches. When my mom got home, she tried to convince me to stop, but I didn't listen. I had already made two bushes look nice, and soon she would see how good it would look. 'You didn't get your stubbornness from me,' she said, but then she brought me juice and kissed my cheek, which made me happy. By the end of the day, I had made a big mess. But I thought that sometimes you need a mess to organize things. That's what I told myself when I went to bed very tired that night. The next day, I was making even more mess when I heard a deep voice say, 'That's quite a project, young lady.' It was Bryce's grandfather. I had seen him before, but only from a distance or behind a window. It felt like a TV character had stepped out of the screen to talk to me.

He said, 'I know we've seen each other sometimes. I'm sorry I didn't introduce myself sooner. I'm Chester Duncan, Bryce's grandfather. And you are Julianna Baker.' He offered his hand, so I removed my glove and shook his big hand. 'Nice to meet you, Mr. Duncan,' I said. He looked much bigger than when I saw him through the window. Suddenly, he took out his gloves and clippers and asked if I was trimming everything to the same height. I wasn't sure anymore. He suggested not removing them because they were special shrubs and started to trim them himself. At first, I was unsure about his help, but he seemed to enjoy it. Then I realized why he came to help after remembering what Bryce said about our yard. He noticed my worried face and asked if he trimmed too much. I said no. He wondered why I looked upset and said he just wanted to help. I told him I wanted to do it by myself.

He laughed and said, 'Oh, I have no doubt about that,' and continued clipping. 'You know, Julianna, I read about you in the newspaper, and I've been living across the street from you for over a year. It's clear that you are very skilled.' We both worked in silence for a little while, but I started to throw the clippings into the pile with more and more force. Soon, I couldn't take it anymore. I turned to him and said, 'You're here because you feel guilty about the eggs, right? Well, our eggs are just fine! We've been eating them for almost three years and no one has gotten sick. Mrs. Stueby and Mrs. Helms are healthy too, and if you didn't want the eggs, you should have just told me!' His hands dropped to his sides and he shook his head, saying, 'Eggs? Poisoned? Julianna, I don't understand what you mean.' Inside, I felt so angry, hurt, and embarrassed that I didn't feel like myself. 'I'm talking about the eggs I've been bringing to your house for more than two years—eggs from my chickens that I could have sold! Eggs that your family has been throwing away!' I yelled at him. I had never yelled at an adult like that before. His voice became very soft. 'I'm sorry. I didn't know about any eggs. Who did you give them to?' 'Bryce!' I choked up as I said his name again. 'Bryce.' Mr. Duncan nodded slowly and said, 'Well,' then he went back to pruning his bush. 'That probably explains it.' 'What do you mean?' He sighed. 'The boy still has a lot to learn.' I just looked at him, not trusting myself to speak.

He said, 'Oh, he's a very good-looking boy, just like his father,' and he looked unhappy. He broke off a branch. I asked, 'Mr. Duncan, why are you here if you think I don't need help and you're not sorry about the eggs?' He looked at me seriously and replied, 'Because you remind me of my wife.' 'Your wife?' I asked. He smiled a bit and said, 'Yes, Renée would have sat in the tree with you all night.' Hearing this, I wasn't angry anymore. 'Really?' I asked. 'Yes,' he said, 'She has passed away.' He nodded and said he missed her a lot. He threw a branch onto a pile and laughed, saying, 'A strong woman makes life better.' I never thought I would be friends with Bryce's grandfather, but by dinner, I felt like I had known him for a long time because of all his stories about his wife and their adventures. His stories also made our work feel easier. When I finished for the night, I had trimmed all the bushes, and despite the big pile of branches, the yard looked much better.

The next day he came back. When I greeted him with a smile and said, 'Hi, Mr. Duncan,' he smiled too and replied, 'Call me Chet, will you?' He noticed the hammer I was holding and asked, 'Are we starting on the fence today?' Chet showed me how to straighten a line for the fence slats, how to grip a hammer at the end of the handle, how to space the slats properly, and how to use a level to make sure the slats were straight up and down. We spent several days working on the fence and talked the whole time. We didn't just talk about his wife. He was also interested in the sycamore tree and understood when I explained that the whole is greater than the sum of its parts. He mentioned that it's the same with people, but sometimes with people, the whole is less than the sum of the parts. I found that very interesting. The following day at school, I looked at my classmates, whom I've known since elementary school, trying to decide if they were more or less than the sum of their parts. Chet was right; many of them were less. At the top of the list was Shelly Stalls. She seems to have everything, but there isn't much substance beneath her very big hair. Although she draws people to her easily, they soon realize that being her friend means constantly boosting her huge ego. But there was one classmate, Bryce, whom I couldn't figure out. Until recently, I was sure he was much more than the sum of his parts. What he did to my heart felt like magic. But now, I don't understand it. And when I saw him in math class and he smiled at me, my heart jumped, but I was also upset with myself for feeling this way.

Why did I still feel upset after what he did? I stayed away from him all day. By the end of school, I felt very mixed up inside. I rode my bike home very fast. The bike made a lot of noise and felt like it might break. When I got home, I still felt upset, so I decided to paint. I opened a can of paint and started painting. About ten minutes later, Chet came by. He noticed I was full of energy and asked why. I told him I was angry, especially at myself. He offered to help paint and asked who I was mad at. I asked him how he fell in love with his wife instead of answering. He joked about me having boy troubles, but I denied it. He shared that he fell in love with his wife by accident, even though he was engaged to someone else at the time. He realized he had been mistaken before it was too late.

Yes, my future wife was very beautiful. She had lovely brown eyes and skin like an angel. At first, all I noticed was her beauty. But later, I realized she was not as good a person as Renée. He dipped his brush in a coffee can and painted a fence. 'Looking back, it's easy to see and give advice, but sadly, many people don't look deeper until it's too late.' We were silent for a while, but I could tell Chet was thinking hard. It wasn't about my issues. 'I'm sorry to mention your wife,' I said. 'Oh, it's okay,' he replied, trying to smile. 'I wasn't thinking about Renée. I was thinking about someone else. Someone who doesn't look deeper. I don't think I want her to anymore.' Who was he talking about? I was curious but felt it was wrong to ask, so we kept painting in silence. Finally, he said, 'Look beyond his eyes, smile, and shiny hair. See what's really there.' His words made me shiver. It felt like he knew something. Was he saying his grandson wasn't good enough? When it was time for dinner, I still felt uneasy, but the worst feeling had passed. Mom said Dad was working late, and since my brothers were out, it was just us. She said they felt weird about Chet coming over like this. Maybe they should pay him for his help. I thought Chet would be offended, but the next day Mom offered anyway. Chet said, 'No, Mrs. Baker. I'm happy to help your daughter with her project,' and refused to discuss it further.

The week ended with my dad putting all the garden waste in his truck before going to work on Saturday morning. Then Chet and I spent the day pulling out weeds and preparing the soil for planting seeds. On this last day, Chet asked me if my family was moving. I was surprised and asked him why he thought so. He told me his daughter mentioned at dinner that we might be selling the house because we were fixing it up. Although Chet and I had talked about many things, I hadn't planned to tell him about Mr. Finnegan, Uncle David, or why our yard was messy until he asked about moving. But after he asked, I ended up telling him everything, and it felt good to share, especially about Uncle David. It was like letting go of a dandelion and watching the seeds fly away. I felt proud of my parents and myself when I looked at the front yard. I was excited to start working on the backyard and even thought about painting the house. I believed I could do it. After I shared my story, Chet was quiet. When my mom brought us sandwiches for lunch, we ate on the porch in silence. Then Chet broke the silence by nodding towards a house across the street and wondered why that person didn’t just say hello. I asked who he meant, and then saw the curtain in Bryce's room move. I asked if he meant Bryce, and Chet said he had seen him watching us three times. My heart started racing like a little bird trying to fly.

He made a face and said, 'Let's finish and plant the seed, okay? The warmth of the day will help it grow.' I was excited to plant the yard, but I kept getting distracted by Bryce's window. Was he watching us? I found myself checking many times that afternoon. I think Chet noticed too, because after we finished and were happy with our work, he said, 'He might be scared now, but I still have hope for him.' Scared? What could I even say? I just stood there holding the hose. Then, Chet said goodbye and walked across the street. A bit later, I saw Bryce walking towards his house. I was surprised. I thought he was inside the whole time, but he was outside? I felt silly. I turned away from him and focused on watering the yard. I felt so foolish! Just then, I heard, 'It looks good, Juli. Nice job.' It was Bryce, standing on our driveway. Suddenly, I wasn't mad at myself anymore. I was mad at him. How could he act like he was in charge and say, 'Nice job?' He shouldn't have said anything after what he did. I was about to spray him with the hose when he said, 'I'm sorry for what I did, Juli. It was wrong.' I looked at him—into his bright blue eyes. I tried to see past them, like Chet suggested. What was he really thinking? Was he truly sorry, or just upset about what he had said? But it was too hard to keep looking at him, like staring at the sun, so I had to look away.

I can't really remember what we talked about, but he was kind and made me laugh. After he left, I turned off the water and went inside feeling very strange. That night, my feelings kept changing from being upset to feeling uneasy. The worst part was, I didn't know why I felt this way. It had something to do with Bryce, but I wasn't just angry or just happy about it. He had come to our house, stood in our driveway, and said nice things. We laughed together. But I wasn't just mad or happy. As I tried to read in bed, I felt more uneasy than upset. I felt like someone was watching me. I was so scared that I checked the window, the closet, and under the bed, but I still felt weird. It was almost midnight when I finally realized what it was. It was me. I was the one watching myself.

On Sunday, I woke up feeling really sick, as if I had a bad dream that made no sense. I've learned that when you have strange and confusing dreams, you should try to forget about them. I managed to forget about the dream and got out of bed early because I was very hungry from not eating much the night before. When I was walking to the kitchen, I saw my dad sleeping on the sofa in the living room. This worried me because it seemed like there were still problems happening, and it made me feel uncomfortable in my own home. My dad moved a bit, made a noise, and then hid under his thin blanket, mumbling something that sounded unfriendly. I went to the kitchen and prepared a big bowl of corn flakes for myself. Just as I was about to add milk, my mom came in and took the bowl from me. She told me that I had to wait because we were going to have breakfast together as a family. I complained that I was very hungry. She told me everyone was hungry and instructed me to take a shower while she made pancakes. Reluctantly, I went to the bathroom, and on my way back, I noticed the living room was tidy again, with everything back in place, making me wonder if I had imagined seeing my dad there. At breakfast, my dad looked normal, not like someone who had slept on the couch. He was wearing tennis shorts and a light purple polo shirt, and his hair was neatly styled. I thought his shirt looked a bit odd, but my mom complimented him, saying he looked very nice. My dad just looked at her with suspicion.

Then my grandfather entered and said, 'Patsy, the house smells great! Good morning, Rick. Hi, Bryce,' and he winked at me as he sat and put his napkin on his lap. 'Lyn-et-ta!' my mother called out. 'Breakfast!' My sister came in wearing a very short skirt and tall shoes, and her eyes looked like a raccoon's. My mother was shocked, but then she took a deep breath and said, 'Good morning, dear. You're... you're... weren't you going to church with your friends this morning?' 'I am,' Lynetta replied with a frown and sat down. Mom brought pancakes, fried eggs, and hash browns to the table. My dad just sat very still for a while, but then he shook out his napkin and put it around his neck. 'Well,' my mother started as she sat down, 'I have thought of a way to solve our problem.' 'Here it comes...' my dad whispered, but a look from my mom made him stop talking. 'The solution is...,' my mom said as she took some pancakes, '...we are going to invite the Bakers for dinner.' My dad exclaimed, 'What?' Lynetta asked, 'All of them?' I said, 'Are you serious?' But my grandfather just added another fried egg to his plate and said, 'That, Patsy, is a wonderful idea.' 'Thanks, Dad,' she replied with a smile, then told Lynetta and me, 'Yes, I'm serious, and if Juli and the boys want to come, they're invited too.' My sister laughed. 'Do you realize what you're saying?' Mom smoothed her napkin on her lap. 'Maybe it's time I found out.' Lynetta turned to me and said, 'She's inviting the worst people over for dinner—oh, this is really something I expected to happen when I woke up!' My dad shook his head and said, 'Patsy, why are we doing this? So I said some silly things last night. Is this how you punish me now?' 'It's something we should have done a long time ago.'

"Patsy, please. I know you're upset about what you learned, but a weird dinner won't fix it!" My mom poured syrup on her pancakes, closed the syrup bottle, licked her finger, and then looked at my dad. "We're having the Bakers over for dinner." And she meant it. Dad sighed deeply and said, "Whatever you want, Patsy. Just remember I warned you." He ate some hash browns and quietly asked, "A barbecue, maybe?" "No, Rick. A formal sit-down dinner. Like when your clients visit." He stopped eating. "Do they need to dress up?" Mom looked at him sharply. "I expect you to act like the gentleman I believe you are." Dad went back to his potatoes. It was easier than arguing with Mom. Lynetta ate all the white part of a fried egg and nearly a whole pancake. She was plain but happy, laughing as she ate. Granddad ate a lot, even for him, but he looked very serious. I started to worry that this dinner might cause problems. It felt like trouble was coming. Granddad knew, but no one else in my family did. What if they talked about it during dinner? I would be in big trouble. Later, while brushing my teeth, I thought about paying Juli to help keep the dinner calm. Or maybe I could stop the dinner from happening. I stopped, looked at myself in the mirror, and wondered why I was being so scared. I spit and went to talk to my mom.

"What's wrong, dear?" she asked while cleaning the griddle. "You seem worried." I checked to make sure no one was around, then whispered, "Can you keep a secret?" She chuckled, "I'm not sure." I waited quietly. She stopped cleaning and looked at me, "This seems serious. What is it?" It had been a long time since I last shared something with my mom. I used to handle things by myself. But now, I needed to talk. She touched my arm, "Bryce, what's bothering you?" I sat on the counter, took a deep breath, and said, "It's about Juli's eggs." "Her eggs?" she asked. "Yes. Do you remember the chicken and salmonella problem?" "That was long ago, but yes." "Well, Juli has been bringing eggs here every week, not just once." "Really? Why didn't I know?" "I was scared Dad would be upset because we didn't want the eggs, so I would take them from Juli before she could ring our doorbell and throw them away." "Oh, Bryce!" "I thought she would stop bringing them. How long can chickens keep laying eggs?" "So, they've stopped?" "Yes, last week. Juli saw me throwing away the eggs." "Oh no." "Yes." "What did you say to her?" I looked down and quietly said, "I told her we were scared of getting sick from salmonella because their yard was messy. She left crying, and then she started cleaning up their yard." "Oh, Bryce!" "Exactly."

She was very quiet for a minute; then she spoke very softly, 'Thank you for being honest, Bryce. It helps me understand a lot.' She shook her head and said, 'I wonder what that family thinks of us,' and then she went back to cleaning the griddle. 'We should invite them for dinner,' I whispered. 'You won't tell anyone about the egg situation, right? I mean, Juli told Granddad, so he knows, but I don't want our dad to find out.' She looked at me for a moment, then said, 'Promise me you've learned your lesson, honey.' 'I have, Mom.' 'Okay, then,' she replied. I sighed with relief. 'Thanks.' 'Oh, and Bryce?' 'Yes?' 'I'm glad you told me about it.' She kissed me on the cheek, smiled, and said, 'Didn't you say you'd mow the lawn today?' 'Right,' I replied, and went outside to mow the lawn. That evening, my mom said the Bakers would come over for dinner on Friday at six o'clock. The dinner would have poached salmon, crab risotto, and steamed vegetables. She insisted that we all must be there. My dad suggested barbecuing instead because he wanted something to do, but my mom stared him down, and he stopped talking about it. So, the Bakers were coming. Seeing Juli at school became more awkward. She didn't show off or anything. Instead, she avoided me. She would say hi if we bumped into each other, but she wasn't around much. She seemed to take hidden paths around the school. I found myself watching her in class. While the teacher was talking and everyone else was paying attention, I kept looking at Juli. It was strange. One moment I'd be listening to the teacher, and the next, I'd just be staring at Juli.

It was only on Wednesday during math class that I realized something. When I saw how her hair fell over her shoulders and how she tilted her head, she reminded me of someone from a newspaper picture. She wasn't exactly the same — the position was different, and her hair wasn't blowing — but she still looked very similar to the photo. Realizing this made me feel a shiver. I wondered what she was thinking about. Was she really interested in math problems? Darla Tressler saw me looking at her and gave me a very mischievous smile. I had to act quickly to avoid gossip, so I pretended there was a bee in her hair and pointed around to distract Darla. Darla started looking for the bee, and I focused on my work for the rest of the day to avoid any trouble with her. Later that night, while doing homework, I checked a newspaper article from the trash to confirm my earlier thought was just my imagination. But there she was, the girl from my math class, looking just like she did in class, printed on the newspaper. Suddenly, Lynetta came into my room wanting my sharpener. I quickly hid the newspaper under my binder and told her she should have knocked. As she was still in my room, I quickly put the binder in my backpack. She teased me, asking what I was hiding. I got annoyed and told her to stop calling me 'baby brother' and to not enter my room like that again. I gave her the sharpener, and she left. Just then, my mom called me, and I got distracted, forgetting about the newspaper in my binder.

The next morning, I was still worried. What could I do with it? I couldn't just throw it away because Garrett was sitting next to me. Also, Darla Tressler was in the class, and she was watching for bees. If she noticed, I would be in trouble. Then, Garrett tried to take a piece of paper, but I accidentally hit his hand hard. 'Dude!' he said. 'What's wrong with you?' 'Sorry,' I replied, realizing he just wanted some lined paper, not the newspaper. 'Dude,' he said again, 'you seem distracted lately. Has anyone else told you that?' He took a paper from my binder and saw the newspaper. He looked at me, and before I could stop him, he pulled it out. I grabbed it back from him, but he had already seen the picture. Before he could speak, I told him to be quiet and that he was wrong about what he thought. 'Relax, I wasn't thinking anything bad,' he said, but I saw he was curious. Then he teased, 'I bet you have a good reason for having Juli Baker's picture.' I was scared by how he said it, like he might tease me in front of everyone. I told him to be quiet. The teacher told us to be quiet too, but Garrett kept teasing. After class, Darla acted like nothing was wrong, but she was watching us. She followed me all day, so I couldn't explain to Garrett. What could I say to him? That I had the paper in my binder to keep it from my sister? That wouldn't really help.

I didn't want to tell a silly lie about it. I really wanted to talk to Garrett because he was my friend and I had a lot on my mind from the past few months. I thought talking to him might help me feel better and stop worrying about things. Garrett was always good at helping with that. During social studies, our class went to the library to research for a project about famous historical figures. Darla and Juli were there too, but I managed to take Garrett to a quiet corner of the library without them seeing us. As soon as we were alone, I started talking to Garrett about chickens. He was confused and asked, 'What are you talking about?' I reminded him of the time we looked over a fence back in sixth grade. I asked him if he remembered how he didn't understand my questions about a hen back then. He sighed and said, 'Not this again.' I told him he didn't know anything about chickens back then and joked that I trusted him too much. Then I told him about my dad, the eggs, and salmonella, and how I had been secretly dealing with eggs for nearly two years. Garrett just nodded and said it made sense. I then blurted out that Juli had caught me. 'Who?' he asked. 'Juli!' I explained. I told him about a recent conversation with her and how she started cleaning her yard aggressively right after. 'So what? It's not your fault her yard is messy,' he said. But then I learned that her family doesn't own their house and they are poor because they are helping her dad's brother who has special needs. Garrett smiled and made an insensitive comment, which shocked me. I was so upset and angry, and for the first time in a long while, I felt like hitting someone.

We were in the library. Also, I thought if I hit him for what he said, he would tell everyone that I liked Juli Baker, which was not true! So I just laughed and said, 'Oh, right,' and then I made an excuse to move away from him. After school, Garrett invited me to his house to hang out, but I didn't want to go. I was still very angry with him. I tried to calm myself down, but I was really mad at him. He had gone too far this time. And it was even worse because my dad was standing right next to him, on the other side.

Sunday mornings are very quiet at our home. My dad sleeps late. My mom doesn't make breakfast. If my brothers are out late with their band, they sleep until noon. I usually get eggs quietly while everyone sleeps, then eat Cheerios in my room and read. But this Sunday, after a night of feeling bad, I woke up wanting to move. I wanted to climb my tree, but I just watered the garden and tried to think of other things. I turned on the water and liked how the dark soil looked as I watered it. I talked to the seeds in the ground, hoping they would grow, when my dad came out. His hair was wet from a shower, and he was holding a bag. 'Dad! Did I wake you?' 'No, I've been awake,' he said. 'Are you going to work?' 'No, I'm going to see Uncle David.' He was walking to his truck. 'But why today? It's Sunday.' 'It's a special Sunday. It's his fortieth birthday. I want to give him a gift,' he said, showing the bag. 'Don't worry. I'll make pancakes for lunch.' 'I'm coming with you,' I said, and dropped the hose. I was just in sweats and sneakers, no socks, but I decided I was going.

Why don't you stay home and enjoy the morning with your mom? I went to the side of his truck and said, 'I'm coming,' then got in and closed the door loudly. 'But—' he started to say. 'I'm coming, Dad.' He looked at me, then said, 'Okay,' and put the bag on the seat. 'I'll write a note to your mom.' While he was inside, I put on my seat belt and thought this was a good thing. I should have done this long ago. Uncle David was part of our family. It was time I got to know him better. I looked at the paper bag next to me. What did my dad get for his brother's fortieth birthday? I picked it up. It wasn't heavy like a painting. It also made a soft, shaking sound. I was about to look inside when my dad came back. I put the bag down and sat straight. When he got into the truck, I asked, 'It's okay with you, right?' He just looked at me and started the truck. 'I'm not spoiling your day, am I?' He started the engine and said, 'No, sweetheart. I'm happy you're coming.' We didn't talk much as we drove to Greenhaven. He liked looking at the scenery, and I had many questions I didn't want to ask yet. But, it felt good to be with my dad. The quiet made us feel closer in a way that talking couldn't. When we got to Greenhaven, we stayed in the truck for a bit. 'It takes time to get used to, Julianna, but you'll start to like it. They're all nice people.' I nodded but felt a bit scared.

"Let's go inside," he said, and took the bag from the seat. Greenhaven didn't look like a hospital or a house to me. It was too long and shaped like a rectangle. The path to the door had a green cover that was old and there were flower beds with new but dirty pansies that were not straight. The lawn had spots with no grass and three big holes near the building. "The people living here take care of the garden," my dad explained. "It helps them as part of their job training, and it's good for them. Those holes are where Peach, Plum, and Pear trees will go." "Fruit trees?" I asked. "Yes, choosing them caused a big fuss," he replied. "Among the people living here?" "Exactly," he said, and opened the glass doors. "Come inside." Inside, it was cool and smelled like pine cleaner and bleach, but also something else strong. There was no reception or place to sit, just a big crossroad with white walls and small wooden benches. On the left was a big room with a TV and many plastic chairs. To the right were offices that were open, and next to us were two big wooden cabinets. One was open with some gray sweaters hanging neatly. "Good morning, Robert!" a woman shouted from one office. "Good morning, Josie," my father answered. She came out and said, "David's been up since six. It's his birthday today, Mabel told me." "Mabel is right," he said, and smiled at me. "Josie, I'm happy to introduce my daughter, Julianna. Julianna, this is Josie Gruenmakker." "How nice," Josie said, shaking my hand. "I've seen you in David's photos. You're about to start high school, right?" I looked at my dad, surprised. I hadn't thought about it like that, but he had. "Yes, I guess I am."

Josie is the boss of this place. She said with a laugh, 'I'm not leaving! I've been here for seventeen years and I'm staying.' Then the phone rang and she left quickly, saying, 'I need to answer that. I'll see you soon. Check the game room, then his room. You'll find him there.' My dad took me around a corner, and as we walked down the hall, there was a strong, bad smell. It smelled like it had been there for years. We saw a small person in a wheelchair. At first, I thought it was a child, but it was a woman as we got closer. She had almost no hair and smiled at my dad without any teeth. She took his hand and tried to speak. Her words were not clear, and I couldn't understand her, but she looked at my dad like he knew what she was saying. To my surprise, my dad understood her and said, 'You're right, Mabel. That's why I'm here today.' He showed a bag of groceries and whispered, 'I brought him a small present.' 'Really?' she said. 'How did you know?' She made a happy noise until my dad comforted her by patting her hand and said, 'I guess I'm easy to predict. But he likes these gifts.' Then he saw her looking at me. 'This is my daughter, Julianna. Julianna, this is Mabel. She remembers everyone's birthdays and loves strawberry milkshakes.' I smiled and said softly, 'Nice to meet you,' but she just looked at me doubtfully. 'We're going to see David now,' my dad said and shook the bag. 'Don't tell him about the surprise if you see him.' I followed my dad to a bedroom door. He stopped and called, 'David? David, it's Robert.'

A man came to the door. He was my uncle, but I didn't know he was my father's brother. He was a big man with thick brown glasses and a pale, puffy face. He hugged my father tightly and shouted happily. My father called him 'little brother.' We went into a room that had walls covered with puzzles glued everywhere, even on the ceiling. It felt cozy and interesting, like being inside a quilted cave. My father showed me to my uncle and said, 'Look who I brought!' My uncle looked scared for a moment, but then smiled big when he saw it was me, Julianna. He shouted my name and hugged me so tight I could barely breathe. He was very happy and laughed. Then he sat down and said it was his birthday. I wished him a happy birthday. He laughed and thanked us. My dad gave him a birthday gift. It was a puzzle, I guessed it by the sound it made. My dad showed it was a puzzle and a pinwheel wrapped in blue paper with a pinwheel on top. My uncle loved the pinwheel and blew on it hard. He shouted 'Orange!' as he did. My dad gently took it and agreed that red and yellow make orange. My dad promised to take it outside later for him to enjoy the wind. He gave the puzzle back to my uncle to hold.

The wrapping paper was all torn up on the floor. I looked closely to see the puzzle my dad got him and was surprised. It had three thousand pieces and was just a picture of white clouds and blue sky, nothing else. My dad pointed at the ceiling and said it would look good there. Uncle David agreed, grabbed his toy, and asked if we could go outside. 'Sure. How about we get some birthday ice cream at McElliot's?' Uncle David was happy and nodded a lot. We left the house with Josie and walked down the street. David walks slowly because his body curves in and he leans a lot on my dad. He was holding his toy and shouting 'Orange, orange!' as it spun. McElliot's was a drugstore with an ice cream section. It had a red and white striped sign and little tables with matching wallpaper. It looked nice for a place inside a drugstore. Dad bought us ice cream cones. We sat down and Dad and David talked a little, but David mostly enjoyed his ice cream. Dad smiled at me sometimes, and I smiled back, but I felt a bit left out. I wondered how many times they had done this without me. How many birthdays had they celebrated? How long had Dad known all these people? It felt like Dad had another family that I didn't know about.

I didn't like it and didn't understand it. I was getting upset when David's ice cream cone broke in his hand and the ice cream fell on the table. Before my dad could do anything, David tried to put the ice cream back on the broken cone. It fell again, this time on the floor. My dad told David to leave it and that he would get him a new one, but David didn't listen. He pushed his chair back and went after the ice cream. My dad tried to stop him, saying he would get a new one, but David kept trying to fix his cone. When his cone broke completely, David started to cry loudly. It was really bad. He was acting like a little kid, crying and shouting. My dad tried to calm him down, then asked me to get another cone. The man at the counter made a new cone quickly, but meanwhile, David knocked over a table and chairs and made a mess with the ice cream. The people at the store were scared and watched David like he was a monster. I brought the new cone to my dad, who gave it to David on the floor. While David ate, we cleaned up the mess. On the way home, David acted as if nothing had happened. He played with his toy and said 'Orange!' sometimes. When we got home, my dad opened the door and David seemed tired. In his room, David put his toy on his bed and took out a puzzle. My dad suggested he rest first, but David wanted to start the puzzle right away. My dad helped him set it up.

My dad took a small table from under the bed, opened its legs, and set it up near the bed. He then placed a chair next to it and told me, 'There you are. All set up.' David had already opened the box and was looking at the pieces. 'Aaaas a gou wwwone, Wobbad.' 'I'm happy you like it. Do you think you can finish it by Wednesday? I can help you hang it up then,' I said. David nodded, focusing on the puzzle and arranging the pieces on the table. My dad patted him on the shoulder and said, 'I'll see you on Wednesday, okay?' David nodded again. 'Can you say goodbye to Julianna?' 'Baaawye,' he replied, without looking up from his puzzle. 'See you later, Uncle David,' I said, trying to sound happy even though I was sad. When we got back into the truck, my dad buckled his seat belt and just said, 'So.' I tried to smile at him. 'Are you as tired as I am?' he asked. I nodded. 'Everything was fine—except for the ice cream,' I said. Dad laughed, 'Except for the ice cream.' Then he became serious. 'The problem is, you never know what will upset him. It could be a fly, or how his socks feel. It's hard to guess. Usually, getting ice cream is easy,' he said, shaking his head and closing his eyes, deep in thought. Then he started the truck and said, 'David lived with your mom and me before you were born. We thought it was better than putting him in a home, but it wasn't.' 'But overall, today went well....'

He put the car in reverse. 'David has many needs, both emotional and physical. Your mom and I couldn't handle them. Luckily, he's happy here. They teach him to take care of himself—how to dress, bathe, and brush his teeth, how to behave and talk with others. They go on trips, and he has a job at a doctor's office doing mail work.' 'He does?' 'He goes there every weekday morning to fold letters and stuff envelopes. Greenhaven has been really good for him. He gets a lot of special attention. He has his own room, his own friends, his own life.' After a minute I said, 'But he's part of the family, Dad. And it just doesn't feel right that he's never visited us. Not even on Christmas or Thanksgiving!' 'He doesn't want to, sweetheart. One year your mother and I made him spend Thanksgiving with us, and it was a huge mess. He got so upset that he broke a car window.' 'But ... why haven't we visited him? I know you have, but the rest of us. Why not?' 'Well, it's tough. Your mother finds it very sad, and I get that. We both thought it wasn't a good place for young kids.' He drove onto the highway, quiet at the wheel. Then he said, 'The years just fly by, Julianna. One day you're holding a baby, and the next, she's almost grown up.' He looked at me with a sad smile. 'I love David, but he is a burden, and I wanted to protect you from that. But I see now how it's impacted you and the family.' 'But Dad, it's not—' 'Julianna, what I'm trying to say is I'm sorry. I wanted to do so much for you. All of you. I just didn't realize until now how little I've actually done.'

"That's not true!" "Well, I think you know I try to be good, but honestly, Mr. Loski might be a better husband and father than me. He's home more, he gives more, and he's probably more fun." My dad usually didn't look for praise, but I was surprised he really thought that. "Dad, I don't care about what it looks like, I think you're the best dad ever! And when I get married, I don't want someone like Mr. Loski. I want someone like you." He looked at me, shocked. "Really," he said with a smile. "I'll remember that when you're older." That made the rest of our trip fun. We laughed and talked about many things, but as we got close to home, we kept talking about one thing. Pancakes. But my mom had different ideas. She had been cleaning all morning and didn't want pancakes. "I need something heavier. Like a grilled ham-and-cheese sandwich. With onions," she said. "Lots of onions!" "Cleaning on a Sunday?" my dad asked. "Why were you cleaning?" "Just felt nervous," she replied. "How was it?" "Okay. I'm glad I went." She looked at my dad and then at me. "Well, good," she sighed, then mentioned, "I also cleaned because I got a call from Patsy." "Loski?" my dad asked. "Is something wrong?" My mom brushed some hair away and said, "No... She invited us for dinner on Friday." We stared at her; then I asked, "All of us?" "Yes." I could see my dad wondering: Why? We've lived across the street for years and never got invited. Why now?

My mom could tell something was up. She sighed and told me, 'Robert, I'm not sure why, but she really wanted us to come. She was almost crying, saying she was sorry for not inviting us earlier and that she wanted to get to know us better.' 'What did you say?' I asked. 'I couldn't just say no. She was very kind, and Chet has helped us a lot...' She paused, then added, 'I agreed we should go. It's planned for six o'clock on Friday night.' 'Really?' I asked. She nodded. 'It might be good. A bit odd, but good.' 'Okay,' my dad agreed. 'I'll make sure I'm free on Friday. What about the boys?' 'They don't have any plans or work, but I haven't asked them yet.' 'Are you sure they're okay with us all going there?' my dad wondered. My mom nodded. 'She really wants us to come.' I could see my dad was a bit uneasy about going to the Loskis' for dinner, but it seemed important to my mom. 'Okay,' he said, and started to prepare some food. I spent the afternoon relaxing, reading, and daydreaming. The next day at school, I kept getting distracted, thinking about David. I thought about my grandparents and what they had experienced with a son like him. I also thought a lot about the sycamore tree. At first, I thought it was because I was sad, but then I remembered my mom saying the tree showed endurance. It had grown despite being damaged when it was young. Some people thought it was ugly, but I didn't. Maybe it's all about perspective. Maybe what I see as ugly, others see as beautiful. Like Shelly Stalls. I didn't see anything special about her, but others seemed to adore her. Me-ow.

The whole week was just me floating by until Thursday. On Thursday, our social studies class went to the library to look up information for a project about famous people in history. I picked Susan B. Anthony and her work for women's voting rights. While I was looking for books, Darla Tressler, a classmate, called me over from the end of the bookshelf. I didn't know Darla well, so I turned around to see if she was calling someone else. She waved at me to come over and whispered for me to listen. I could hear Garrett and Bryce talking through the books. They were talking about me, my chickens, and how Bryce had been throwing away the eggs I gave him. They also mentioned how I was fixing up our yard. Bryce seemed upset, but then he mentioned David, which made me very worried. Garrett laughed and insulted me, and Bryce agreed. I felt so shocked that I sat down hard on the floor. The voices stopped, and Darla came to sit by me. She apologized, thinking Bryce was going to say he liked me. I told her Bryce didn't like me. Darla insisted Bryce had feelings for me because she saw him staring at me, saying there was a bee in my hair as an excuse. I joked that with my luck, there probably was a bee in my hair.
Shouldn't I?

Finding out that my dad and Garrett had the same sense of humor really creeped me out. It was very hard for me to even look at my dad, and talking to him was even harder. However, on Friday afternoon around five, I agreed with him that we should have had a barbecue because it's more relaxed. Instead, my mom was very busy in the kitchen, cutting things up and giving orders to my dad and me as if we were preparing for a very important guest. We cleaned the floor, added a part to make the table bigger, brought in five more chairs, and set the table. We didn't set it right, but my mom just moved some things around to fix it. It looked the same to me, but I'm not an expert. She put out some candle holders and asked, 'Rick, can you put the dishes in and start the dishwasher? I want to get ready. After that, you can change. And Bryce? What are you wearing?' 'Mom, they're just the Bakers. Why make them feel bad?' 'Trina and I decided to dress up, so — ' 'But why?' My dad put his hand on my shoulder and said, 'So we all feel equally uncomfortable, son.' I looked at her and asked, 'Do I have to wear a tie?' 'No, but wearing a shirt with buttons instead of a T-shirt would be nice.' I went to my room and searched my closet for a shirt with buttons. There were many buttons, and all of them looked nerdy. I thought about not following my mom's dress rules, but I started trying on shirts anyway. Twenty minutes later, I still wasn't ready. I was very annoyed because why did it matter? Why did I care how I looked for this silly dinner? I was behaving like a girl. Then, through a small opening in my curtains, I saw them coming out of their house, walking down their path, crossing the street. It felt like a strange dream. They seemed to be floating towards our house. All five of them. I grabbed a shirt from my bed, put my arms through, and buttoned it up.

Two seconds after, the doorbell rang and Mom shouted, 'Bryce, can you get that?' Luckily, Granddad opened the door first. He welcomed them as if they were family we hadn't seen in a long time and even knew which twin was Matt and which was Mike. One twin wore a purple shirt and the other a green one, so it should have been easy to remember them, but they confused me by pinching my cheeks and calling me 'baby brother.' I got so annoyed that I forgot who was who again. Mom rushed from the kitchen and welcomed everyone warmly, then called out for Lynetta and Rick to come because we had guests. But she paused when she saw Juli and Mrs. Baker with pies. 'What's this?' she asked. Mrs. Baker replied, 'Homemade pies, blackberry cheesecake, and pecan.' Mom was very excited and said the pies looked amazing. She took Juli's pie and quickly went to the kitchen with Mrs. Baker. Lynetta then came around the corner, and Matt and Mike complimented her looks. They all went to Lynetta's room. Meanwhile, Granddad led Mr. Baker to the front room, leaving me alone in the hallway with Juli. Juli wasn't looking at me; she seemed distracted by everything else. I felt awkward, standing there in my formal shirt with sore cheeks and nothing to talk about. I became so nervous that my heart started beating very fast, like it does before a sports game.

She looked more like the picture in the newspaper than the actual picture, which might sound strange. She wasn't dressed up fancy. She wore a simple dress and shoes, and her hair looked normal, maybe a bit more combed than usual. She stood with confidence, not looking at me, but around with sharp eyes. We stood there for only a few seconds, but it felt much longer. Then I greeted her, 'Hi, Juli.' Her eyes sparkled with anger, and I realized she was upset. She whispered angrily that she heard me and my friend Garrett mocking her uncle in the library, and she didn't want to talk to me ever again. I was confused, wondering where she had been because I didn't see her near us in the library. Maybe someone else told her what happened. I tried to explain it was all Garrett's doing, not mine, but she didn't listen and walked away to find her dad. There I was, regretting not confronting Garrett earlier, when my dad came over and asked how I was enjoying the party. I didn't really want to talk to him. He looked into the room and complimented Juli's dad on his appearance. I corrected my dad that his name was Robert. My dad acted like he knew that and said he should go greet him. I said I might need to help my mom instead. But I didn't go to help; I just watched as my dad and Mr. Baker shook hands, both smiling. Watching them, I felt strange again, not about Juli this time, but about my dad who seemed smaller standing next to Mr. Baker.
