Mr. Baker's jaw looked strong, but my dad's face seemed weak in comparison. It's sad to feel this way about your own father. When I was younger, I believed my dad was the strongest and always right. However, now I saw Mr. Baker and thought he could easily defeat my dad. Even worse was how my dad behaved. He was pretending to be friendly with Juli's dad, and it looked like he was lying to everyone. Why couldn't he just be himself and act politely without pretending? It was more than just trying to keep my mom happy; it was really upsetting. People often say I look just like my dad, which bothered me now. My mom rang the dinner bell and announced that snacks were ready. She noticed me in the hallway and asked about my sister and the boys. I guessed they were in her room. She asked me to call them to eat. I agreed, eager to distract myself. I went to Lynetta's room and instead of knocking, I just walked in. Did Lynetta get mad? No, she just ignored me. Matt and Mike acknowledged me, and Lynetta noticed me but kept listening to her music. Matt or Mike quietly said they'd be done soon, as if I was obviously there to call them for dinner.

Something made me feel left out, like I was not important to those guys. I was just the little brother. This was not new, but now it bothered me a lot. Suddenly, I felt I didn't belong anywhere. Not at school, not at home. Every time I looked around, people I knew well seemed like strangers to me. I even felt like a stranger to myself. Standing around, eating small crackers with cheese and fish eggs did not make me feel better. My mom was very busy, moving around a lot, serving drinks, and explaining the food without eating any. Lynetta did not like the explanation about the snacks and thought they were gross, disgusting, and revolting. Being near her did not stop the Baker boys from quickly eating lots of crackers. I just watched them, expecting them to do something silly like hug a table leg. Juli, her dad, my grandfather, and my dad were all talking with others, and I felt as silly as my dad looked, standing alone. My mom came over and asked if I was okay. I said yes, but she still pushed me to join Granddad. She told me dinner was almost ready. So I joined them, but they just continued talking without noticing me. They talked about something called perpetual motion.

My friend, I didn't know what perpetual motion meant. They were discussing things like closed systems, open systems, resistance, energy sources, and magnetism. It felt like they were speaking a different language. And Juli, she was talking about things like reversing the polarity of magnets as if she really understood the conversation. Then her dad and my granddad would explain why her ideas wouldn't work, which only made Juli ask more questions.
I was very confused. Even though I acted like I understood what they were talking about, I was actually trying not to just stare at Juli.
When my mom called us for dinner, I tried to apologize to Juli, but she ignored me, and I couldn't blame her.
I sat down across from her, feeling quite sad. Why hadn't I stood up to Garrett in the library? I didn't need to hit him, but why didn't I tell him he was wrong?
After Mom gave everyone their dinner, Dad decided to lead the conversation. 'So, Mike and Matt,' he said, 'you're seniors this year.' They replied, 'Amen!'
'Amen? Are you happy high school is ending?' 'Definitely.'
My dad played with his fork. 'Why?' Matt and Mike looked at each other, then back at my dad. 'Repeating the same stuff gets old.'

'That's funny,' he said, looking around. 'I loved high school.' Matt or Mike replied, 'Really? Dude, it's so boring!' Mrs. Baker gave him a stern look, but he continued, 'It's true, Mom. It's all about control and conforming. I'm tired of it.'

My dad looks at my mom and smiles in a way that says 'I was right,' then he asks Matt and Mike, 'So, you're not considering college anymore?' Wow, why was he acting like that? Suddenly, I felt tense and held my fork and knife tightly, ready to defend the two guys who used to pinch my cheeks and call me their baby brother. I took a deep breath and tried to calm down. This was not my battle to fight. Besides, Matt and Mike didn't seem bothered. 'Oh, no,' they replied, 'College is still an option.' 'Yes, we were accepted to a few places, but we want to try music first.' 'Oh, the music thing,' my dad commented. Matt and Mike just looked at each other, shrugged, and continued eating. But Lynetta was upset and told him, 'Dad, your sarcasm isn't welcome.' 'Lyn, Lyn,' one of them said, 'It's okay. Everyone reacts like that. It's a matter of showing, not just telling.' 'That's a great idea,' Lynetta exclaimed, and she ran down the hallway. Mom stopped, unsure how to react to Lynetta's behavior, but then Mrs. Baker complimented, 'Dinner is absolutely delicious, Patsy.' 'Thanks, Trina. It's nice having everyone here.' After a brief silence, Lynetta returned and started pressing the CD player buttons until it opened. 'Lyn, no! Not a good idea,' one of the brothers warned. 'Yeah, Lyn. That's not really suitable dinner music.' 'Tough,' Lynetta said, and turned up the music loud.

Boom, whack! Boom-boom, whack! The candles almost fall from their holders; then guitars make a loud noise that almost blows them out. Matt and Mike look at the speakers, smile at each other, and shout to my dad, 'Great surround sound system, Mr. Loski!' All the adults really want to turn the volume down, but Lynetta stops them with a stern look. When the song ends, Lynetta takes out the CD, turns off the player, and then actually smiles at Matt and Mike, saying, 'That song is amazing. I want to hear it over and over.' Matt-or-Mike tells my dad, 'You might not like it, but this is our music.' 'Did you guys write that song?' 'Yes.' He asks Lynetta to give him the CD, asking, 'Only this one song on it?' Matt-or-Mike laughs and says, 'No, we have a thousand songs, but only three are on this demo.' Dad holds up the CD. 'This is the demo?' 'Yes.' He looks at it and asks, 'So if your band is called Piss Poor, how do you manage to make CDs?' 'Dad!' Lynetta scolds him. 'It's fine, Lyn. Just joking, right Mr. Loski?' My dad laughs a bit and agrees, but then he says, 'Still, I am curious. This demo looks professionally made, and I know studio time is usually too expensive for most bands...' Matt and Mike give each other a high five. While I'm worried about my dad asking them about money, my mom is awkwardly trying to change the subject. 'When Rick and I met, he was in a band...' Suddenly, I start choking on my food. Lynetta looks shocked and asks, 'You were in a band? Did you play the clarinet?' 'No, dear,' my mom says, trying to keep calm. 'Your father played the guitar.' 'Guitar?' 'Cool!' Matt-or-Mike says. 'What kind of music? Rock? Country? Jazz?'

"Country," my dad says, meaning it's important. "Which is nothing to laugh at, boys." "Dude! We know. Total respect, man." "Making a demo was super expensive in the big city, even with some competition. Making one here? I didn't even know we could," Matt and Mike still smiling, say, "There's not." "So where did you go? And how could you afford it?" My mom hits him lightly, so he says, "I'm just curious, Patsy!" Matt and Mike lean closer. "We did it ourselves." "You made this by yourselves? That can't be," he says, almost upset. "How did you get the equipment?" My mom nudges him, but Dad asks her to stop because he's just curious. Matt or Mike tells him, "It's okay, Mrs. Loski." He smiles at my dad, "We kept looking online for a good deal. People are selling their old stuff for new digital ones, but we think digital isn't as good. It loses quality, and we like our sound full." My granddad points out, "But a CD is digital, right?" "Yes, but that's the only digital part we use because we have to. Everyone wants CDs. But everything else is old-school analog. We could buy it because we saved money since we were kids." He smiles, "Do you still play? We could record some of your songs if you like." My dad looks unsure, almost emotional, then he just smiles a little and says, "Thanks, but I don't do that anymore."

That was probably the only true thing my dad said all evening. After that, he was silent. He tried to smile sometimes, but he really seemed sad. I felt a bit sorry for him. Maybe he was remembering the times he played music in a band? I imagined him wearing cowboy boots and a hat, playing guitar and singing a song by Willie Nelson. He was right — that image didn't fit him at all. But the fact that it used to be true made me feel even more out of place. Later, when the night ended and the Bakers were leaving, something weird happened. Juli touched my arm. It was the first time she really looked at me that night. She looked at me in a special way. She whispered, 'I'm sorry I was so mad when we first got here. Everyone had fun, and your mom is really nice for inviting us.' I just stood there, not knowing what to say, looking at her. 'Bryce?' she said, touching my arm again. 'Did you hear me? I'm sorry.' I nodded, but my arm felt strange, my heart was beating fast, and I felt drawn to her. Then she left, saying happy goodbyes. I tried to understand my feelings. What was happening to me? My mom closed the door and said, 'See? Didn't I tell you? They're a wonderful family! The boys are not at all what I thought. Lynetta, why didn’t you say they were so charming?' 'They're drug dealers,' my dad said. Everyone was shocked. 'What?' my mom asked. 'There's no way those boys could buy such expensive recording equipment otherwise,' he said, looking at Lynetta. Lynetta looked very surprised.

“Rick, stop!” my mother said. “You can't say things like that!” “It's the only thing that makes sense, Patsy. Trust me, I know what musicians are like. There is no other reason for this.” Lynetta yelled, “I know for sure they don't use or sell. How can you say that? You are so two-faced, rude, and narrow-minded!” Then there was a moment of quiet, and then he hit her, hard, right on the cheek. This made my mother get very angry and face him like never before, and my sister yelled at him as she ran to her room. My heart was beating fast. Lynetta was right and I almost confronted him too, and told him she was right. But then my granddad took me aside and we both went to different parts of the house. Walking in my room, I wanted to go talk to Lynetta. To tell her she was right, and that Dad was very wrong. But I could hear her crying and yelling through the walls while my mom tried to calm her down. Then she left the house suddenly, and my mom went back to talking with my dad. So I stayed in my room. And even though things seemed to calm down by eleven o'clock, I could still feel some tension. Lying in my bed, looking out the window at the sky, I thought about how my dad always disliked the Bakers. How he criticized their house, yard, cars, and jobs. How he called them trash and laughed at Mr. Baker's paintings. And now I realized there was something really cool about that family. They were genuine. And what about us? Something was very wrong inside our house. It felt like seeing the Bakers' life showed us a bad side of our own life. Where did all these problems come from? And why hadn't I noticed them before.

When I got home, I realized it was wrong to skip the Loskis' dinner party. My mom was excited, picking out recipes and clothes. She even got a new shirt for Dad and checked what my brothers and I were going to wear. She was really looking forward to it, and I didn't want to upset her by saying I didn't like Bryce anymore. Dad was already upset about something else, and he didn't need to hear about the silly things some kids at school said. So, I helped Mom bake pies that evening and told myself it was the right choice. It was just one dinner, after all. The next day at school, I tried to stay away from Bryce. Later, as I was getting ready, I looked at a painting my dad gave me and got angry about Bryce again. He was never nice to me. He didn't help with the tree, ruined my eggs, and made fun of me. But I still acted friendly. When it was time to leave for the dinner, I thought about telling my mom I didn't want to go. But she looked so happy, I just couldn't say it. I took a deep breath, grabbed a pie, and walked over with my family. Chet opened the door. I thought I might be mad at him for telling the Loskis about my uncle, but I wasn't. It wasn't his fault, and he wasn't the one teasing.

Mrs. Loski quickly brought Chet and us inside, and she was very busy moving around. She was wearing a lot of makeup, but I could still see the dark circles under her eyes. Then Mrs. Loski and my mom took the pies somewhere, my brothers went off with Lynetta, and my dad followed Chet to the living room. And just like that, I was left alone in the entrance with Bryce. He greeted me, and I got very upset. I turned to him angrily and said, 'Don't talk to me! I heard you and Garrett talking in the library, and I don't want to talk to you now or ever!' I tried to walk away, but he stopped me. 'Juli! Juli, wait!' he said quietly. 'It wasn't me! It was all Garrett!' I looked at him angrily. 'I know what I heard.' 'No, you don't understand!' he replied. 'I felt bad about the eggs and what I said about your yard. I didn't know about your uncle or your family's situation. I just needed to talk to someone.' We looked at each other, and for the first time, his eyes didn't confuse me. 'I heard you laugh when he made a mean joke about me,' I said. 'Juli, I wanted to confront him, but we were in the library...' he explained. 'So you laughed instead,' I said. He looked sad and embarrassed. 'Yes.' I left him there and went to the living room. If he was lying, he was a good actor. If he was honest, then he was being cowardly, as Chet said. Either way, I didn't want to be near him. I stood next to my dad and listened to him talk with Chet about something they read in the newspaper. My dad said, 'What he suggests needs a machine that can run forever, which isn't possible.' Chet answered, 'Maybe with what scientists know now, but who knows about the future?'

At that time, I wasn't interested in science at all. Trying to forget about Bryce Loski, I asked, 'What's a perpetual-motion machine?' My father and Chet looked at each other, laughed, and then shrugged, making me feel like they were letting me in on a secret. My father said, 'It's a machine that works without needing any outside power.' 'No electricity, no fuel, no water power, nothing,' added Chet. He looked past me and asked without much interest, 'Do you think that's possible?' What was distracting him? Was Bryce still near the entrance? Why didn't he leave? I tried to pay attention to what they were saying. 'Do I think it's possible? I'm not sure. All machines need some kind of energy, even the ones that work really well. And the energy has to come from somewhere...' 'What if the machine made its own energy?' Chet suggested, but he was still looking towards the entrance. 'How could it do that?' I asked. They didn't answer. Instead, my father greeted Mr. Loski, who had just arrived, with a handshake. 'Good evening, Rick. Nice to see you.' Mr. Loski shook my dad's hand and started talking with us, first about the weather. When there was nothing left to say about that, he commented, 'Wow, your yard looks great. I told Chet we should hire him. He really knows his stuff, doesn't he?' He was probably joking. But my father and Chet didn't seem to think it was funny. I was worried about what might happen next, but then Mrs. Loski rang a small dinner bell and called out, 'Snacks for everyone!' The snacks were tasty. But when my father whispered that the small black things on the crackers were not berries but caviar, I almost didn't want to eat them. Fish eggs? Gross! Then my father reminded me that I eat chicken eggs all the time, so why be grossed out by fish eggs? He was right. I slowly finished my cracker, and soon I was eating another one.

Bryce was alone on the other side of the room, and every time I looked over, he was looking at me. I turned my back to him and asked my dad, 'Who is trying to make a machine that runs forever?' My dad laughed and said, 'Crazy scientists around the world.' 'Really?' I asked. 'Yes, for hundreds of years,' he replied. 'What do they do? What does such a machine look like?' Soon, Chet joined the talk. Just as I was beginning to understand some science stuff like magnetism and energy, I felt someone behind me. It was Bryce. I felt angry. I wanted to be alone, but when I moved, Bryce joined our group. I thought he wouldn't care about our topic. I kept talking, hoping he would leave. But he stayed and listened. Then, when it was time for dinner, Bryce grabbed my arm and said he was sorry for being mean. I pulled away and told him he's been sorry a lot lately, then I left him. Later, I realized I should have let him apologize without reacting badly. But I didn't, and that made me seem rude.

I quickly looked at him from across the table, but he was watching his dad talk to my brothers about finishing school and their future college plans. I had seen Mr. Loski many times before, but always from far away. Yet, I realized I had never really noticed his blue eyes before. They were very bright blue. His eyes looked a bit deep set and were partly hidden by his eyebrows and cheekbones, but it was clear that Bryce got his eyes from him. Mr. Loski had black hair and white, straight teeth just like Bryce. Although Chet said Bryce looked exactly like his dad, I hadn't thought they were that similar. But now, looking at them, I could see the resemblance, though his dad looked a bit proud while Bryce looked angry. Then, from the other side of the table, I heard, 'Your sarcasm is not appreciated, Dad.' Mrs. Loski gasped a little, and everyone turned to look at Lynetta. She simply said, 'Well, it's not.' I've lived across from the Loskis for many years and have barely spoken to Lynetta. She seems intimidating to me. So, it wasn't surprising to see her upset with her dad, but it still made me feel uneasy. Mrs. Loski tried to keep smiling, but she was blinking a lot and looked nervous. I watched everyone at the table, wondering if dinners at the Loskis' were always so stressful. Suddenly, Lynetta left the table quickly and came back with a CD. She played it, and it was one of my brothers' songs, which we have heard many times at home. I glanced at my mom, worried she might be embarrassed by the loud guitars and rough lyrics in the song. This music was definitely not the fancy type.

She looked a bit unsure but happy. She was smiling secretly with my dad, and I think she even laughed a little. My dad seemed amused but quiet, and by the end of the song, I understood he was proud. Proud of the music his sons made. This was surprising to me. Dad usually didn't show much interest in my brothers' band, though he never spoke badly about it either. Then Mr. Loski started asking Matt and Mike how they managed to pay for their music recording. They talked about working, saving money, and finding good deals on equipment. That's when I realized why my dad was proud. My brothers were also happy, especially because Lynetta kept saying how amazing their band 'Candle Ice' was. She was really excited, which was strange for her. Looking around, I felt like we were eating dinner with people we didn't know, even though they lived across the street for a long time. Lynetta knew how to smile. Mr. Loski looked neat but seemed to be hiding something not so nice. Mrs. Loski looked nervous and very busy. Maybe our visit made her anxious? Then there was Bryce. I realized I didn't really know him, and from what I had found out, I didn't want to. When I looked at him, I felt nothing. No excitement, no anger, nothing.

After we finished dessert and it was time to leave, I approached Bryce and apologized for being too harsh when we arrived. I admitted that I should have let him apologize and appreciated his family hosting us. I mentioned that my mom enjoyed herself, and that was important to me. We looked at each other, but it seemed like he didn't hear me. I repeated my apology. He nodded, then our families said goodbye. I followed my mom, who was with my dad, and walked next to my brothers, who carried leftover pies. In the kitchen, Matt got some milk and told Mike that Mr. Loski was watching us closely tonight. Mike agreed, joking about Mr. Loski's daughter not being of interest to him, but to their friend Skyler. Matt commented that she was impressive that night. My dad served himself some pie, praising us for being calm. Matt joked about not wanting Mr. Loski as a dad, and Mike laughed. Matt then showed appreciation for our dad, and my mom agreed. I had never seen my dad cry, but he had tears in his eyes then. He tried to hide it and offered us pie with our milk. Matt happily accepted.

"Yes," Mike said. "I'm very hungry." "Get me a plate, too!" I shouted as Mike searched the cupboard. "But we just ate," my mother exclaimed. "Come on, Trina, have some pie. It's tasty.",I went to bed that night feeling full and happy. Lying in the dark, I thought about how many feelings a day can hold, and how good it felt to end the day like this. As I got comfortable and started to fall asleep, my heart felt incredibly free. The next morning I still felt happy. I went outside and watered the lawn, enjoying the sound of water hitting the ground, and wondered when the first grass would start to grow. Then I cleaned the chicken coop, raked the yard, and removed some big weeds. Mrs. Stueby, my neighbor, talked to me over the fence as I was throwing away the weeds and asked, "How's it going, Julianna? Cleaning up for a rooster?" "A rooster?" "Yes, the chickens need a rooster to lay more eggs!",It was true that Bonnie, Clydette, and the other chickens were laying fewer eggs than before. But a rooster? "I don't think the neighbors would like it if I got a rooster, Mrs. Stueby. Also, we'd end up with chicks and we can't handle more birds." "That's not true. You've given them a lot of space. They can share it. How else will you keep your egg business going? Soon they won't lay any eggs at all!" "They won't?" "Well, not many." I shook my head and replied, "They were just my baby chicks that grew up and started laying eggs. I never thought about it as a business."

I'm sorry for running a tab and I'll pay you this week. Maybe buy a rooster with the money. My friend loves my deviled eggs but can't make them taste the same. She'd probably buy the secret ingredient from you. Great job on your front yard, Julianna! It looks amazing. I thought about getting a rooster since they help chickens lay more eggs. But I don't really want to deal with that. I'm fine if my chickens stop laying eggs. I put away my tools, kissed my hens, and went inside feeling good about my decisions. But going back to school changed everything.

The Monday after the dinner party at the Loskis', Darla found me at school and made me think about Bryce Loski again. 'Jules! Hey, wait! How are you?' 'I'm good, Darla, how about you?' 'No, really,' she whispered, 'are you okay?' She moved her backpack around and looked around. 'I was thinking about how mean Bryce was to you, especially since you like him.' 'Who told you that?' 'I can see it, it's obvious. That's why I'm worried about you. Are you really okay?' 'Yes, I am. But thanks for worrying about me.' I looked at her and said, 'And Darla? It's not obvious anymore.' She laughed. 'How long will you stop liking him?' 'It's not temporary. I really don't like him anymore.' She looked at me doubtfully. 'Really?' 'Yes, really. But thanks for caring.' During the first class, I felt strong and sure, but then Mrs. Simmons ended the class 15 minutes early and told us, 'Clear your desks, leave only a pen or pencil.' 'What?' everyone shouted, and I was just as surprised. I was not ready for a quiz! 'Hurry up!' she said. 'You're wasting time.' Everyone started moving their stuff and when we were ready, she took a stack of bright yellow papers, smiled wickedly, and said, 'It's time to vote for basket boys!'

Everyone felt relieved in the room. 'Basket boys? So, it's not a test?' She checked the papers, counting them as she talked. 'It's similar to a test because I don't want you talking to each other. Also, you have limited time.' She put a pile of papers on the first desk of the first row, then moved to the second row. 'I will take them from you one by one when the bell rings, and I will check if you followed these rules.' She moved to the third row. 'Pick five, and only five, boys from the list. Do not write your name on it, and do not talk about your choices with others.' She was now in the fourth row, speaking quickly. 'After choosing, just turn your paper over.' She placed the rest of the papers on the last desk. 'Do not fold your paper!' Robbie Castinon raised his hand and said, 'Why do boys have to vote? It's silly.' 'Robbie...,' Mrs. Simmons warned him. 'Really! Are we voting for friends or enemies?' Some classmates laughed, and Mrs. Simmons looked upset, but Robbie made a good point. Twenty boys from eighth grade would have to prepare a picnic for two and be sold to the highest offer. 'Being a basket boy is a privilege—' Mrs. Simmons started, but Robbie interrupted. 'It's a joke!' he exclaimed. 'It's embarrassing! Who would want to be a basket boy?' The other boys said, 'Not me,' but Mrs. Simmons spoke firmly, 'You should be proud! It's a tradition that has supported our school since its beginning. Many basket boys have helped build our school. That's why we have gardens, shady trees, and apple trees.'

Go to another middle school and you'll see how special our school is. 'All this because of the hard work of boys who play basketball,' Robbie said unhappily. Mrs. Simmons sighed. 'Robbie, one day when your kids come here, you'll get it. For now, just vote for the boy you think will get a high offer. And everyone, we only have nine minutes left.' The room became silent. As I looked over the list of more than 150 boys in the eighth grade, I realized I had only ever noticed one boy, Bryce. I didn't let myself feel too sad. I liked him for the wrong reasons and I wasn't going to vote for him. But I didn't know who else to pick. I looked at Mrs. Simmons, who was watching us closely and also the clock. What if I didn't pick anyone? What if I left it blank? She would probably give me detention. So with two minutes left, I marked the boys I knew who were nice, not mean or silly. When I finished, I had marked ten names and circled five: Ryan Noll, Vince Olson, Adrian Iglesias, Ian Lai, and Jon Trulock. They wouldn't be chosen as basket boys, but I wasn't going to bid anyway. When the bell rang, I handed in my vote and forgot about the whole thing. Until the next day at lunch. Darla stopped me on my way to the library and pulled me to her table instead. 'Did you see the list?' she asked. 'What list?' 'The list of basket boys!' She showed me a paper with twenty names and said, 'Look, the boy you like is on it!' Bryce Loski was five names down from the top.

I should have known, but I suddenly felt very jealous. Who chose him? He must have received many votes from the one hundred fifty names! I imagined many girls offering money to the Booster ladies, hoping to have lunch with him.
I gave the list back to Darla and said, 'He's not important to me! In fact, I didn't vote for him.' 'Wow, girl! You're really avoiding him!'
'It's not about avoiding, Darla. I'm... I'm over him, okay?' 'I'm happy to hear that, because I heard that Shelly is trying to be close to him.'
'Shelly? Shelly Stalls?' I felt my face get hot.

'Yes,' Darla said. She waved her list and called, 'Liz! Macy! Come here! I have the list!' Darla's friends rushed over and looked at the paper as if it was very special. Macy said, 'Chad Ormonde is on it! He is so cute. I'd pay ten dollars for him, easy!' 'And Denny is on it, too!' Liz said excitedly. 'That boy is'—she giggled—'amazing!'
Macy looked a bit confused and said, 'Jon Trulock? Jon Trulock? How is he on this list?' I couldn't believe what I heard. I grabbed the paper from Macy. 'Are you sure?'
'Right there,' she pointed at his name. 'Who do you think voted for him?' 'The quiet girls, maybe,' Darla guessed. 'I'm more interested in Mike Abenido. Do I have any rivals?'
Macy laughed, 'If you're going for him, I'm not!' 'Me neither,' said Liz.
'What about you, Jules?' Darla asked me. 'Bringing some money on Friday?' 'No!'
'You'd miss the second half of school...' 'No! I'm not bidding. On anyone!'

She laughed. 'Good for you.' That afternoon, as I rode home from school, I kept thinking about Bryce and the whole auction. I found myself caring about Bryce again. But why should I care if Shelly likes him? I shouldn't even think about him!

When I wasn't thinking about Bryce, I was worried about Jon Trulock. He was quiet, and I felt bad for him because he had to hold a basket and be sold in front of all the students. What had I done to him? But as I rode up to our house, I stopped thinking about the basket boys. Did I see something green in the soil? Yes! I saw it! I left my bike and knelt down. The plants were very thin and small, and spaced far apart! They were hard to see in the big, black soil, but they were there, growing towards the sun. I ran inside and shouted, 'Mom! Mom, there's grass!' 'Really?' she replied, coming out of the bathroom with her cleaning gloves and a bucket. 'I wondered if it would ever grow.' 'It has! Come and see!' At first, she wasn't very excited. But when I made her kneel down and look closely, she smiled and said, 'They're so delicate.' 'They look like they're yawning, right?' I asked. She tilted her head and looked again. 'Yawning?' 'Well, more like stretching, I think. Like they're waking up in their dirt bed, stretching their arms up high, and saying, Good morning, world!' She laughed and agreed, 'Yes, they do!' I stood up and grabbed the hose. 'I think they need a morning shower, don't you?' My mom agreed and left me to water and sing. I was enjoying watering the new grass when I heard the school bus stop nearby. Bryce. His name made me panic. I couldn't help it; I ran inside and locked myself in my room. I tried to do my homework. Where was my calm? Where was my determination? Where was my sanity? Had they gone because Shelly Stalls was after Bryce? Was it just an old rivalry making me feel this way?

I needed to avoid Bryce and Shelly because they were perfect for each other. Deep down, I felt like I wasn't strong enough yet, like new grass that can't be stepped on. So, I decided to keep my distance from him and remove him from my life. I ignored all the gossip about the popular boys and avoided Bryce at school. If we did meet, I just greeted him casually, as if he was a stranger. This strategy was making me stronger every day, and I didn't care about the school events or the popular boys anymore! One Friday morning, I got up early, did some chores, and prepared for school. While getting ready, I thought of Shelly Stalls because it was the day of the auction, and she was probably styling her hair extravagantly from early morning. I shrugged it off, but paused when I was about to leave, thinking about my savings. I decided against using them and rushed to get my bike from the garage. Just as I was leaving, Mrs. Stueby stopped me to give me some money she owed. I didn't know how much it was, and at that moment, I didn't want to take it. She insisted and even put the money in my pocket, telling me to buy something nice like a rooster, then she went back to her house.

I called out, 'Mrs. Stueby... Mrs. Stueby?' because I didn't want a rooster, but she had already left. On my way to school, I kept thinking about the money Mrs. Stueby gave me. How much money was it? When I arrived at school, I parked my bike and checked the money. I counted ten, fifteen, sixteen, seventeen, eighteen dollars. I put the money back in my pocket. Did I have more money than Shelly? During the first class, I was upset with myself for thinking about the money. In the second class, I tried not to look at Bryce, even though he looked nice in a tie and cuff links. During the break, Shelly Stalls suddenly came up to me at my locker. She asked if I was going to bid on Bryce. I was surprised and denied it. She mentioned seeing me with a lot of money earlier. I told her it was none of her business and that I wasn't interested in bidding. I didn't like Bryce anymore. She laughed, but I told her it was true and that she could waste her money if she wanted. I didn't care. I walked away, leaving her surprised, which felt good. This good feeling lasted until eleven o'clock when everyone gathered in the gym. I decided I wouldn't bid on Bryce. But then Bryce came on stage, looking cute holding a picnic basket. Seeing him made me feel nervous about Shelly winning the bid. Then Darla whispered to me, asking if I really had a lot of money. I was confused but admitted I had the money, though I insisted I wasn't going to bid. Darla noticed I looked unwell. I felt sick and shaky, but I told her I was fine.

She looked at me, then at the stage, and back at me again. 'You have nothing to lose except your self-respect.' 'Stop it!' I said to her quietly but with strong feeling. I felt like I was starting to panic. I had trouble breathing. I felt dizzy and unstable, as if I couldn't control my body. Darla suggested, 'Maybe you should sit down.' 'I'm okay, Darla, really,' I replied. She looked worried and said, 'I'll stay here just to be sure.' The head of the Booster Club, Mrs. McClure, was busy with the basket boys, fixing their ties and giving last instructions. Suddenly, she hit her gavel on the stand and spoke into the microphone, 'Please, everyone, calm down, we're about to start.' I had never seen six hundred students become quiet so quickly. Mrs. McClure seemed surprised too, and she thanked everyone. Then she announced, 'Welcome to the fifty-second Basket Boy Auction! Your teachers have told you the rules in homeroom, but I need to remind you: behave properly. No whistling or rude noises. If you want to make a bid, raise your hand high. You must not bid without raising your hand, and no jokes, or you'll be caught and possibly suspended. Everyone clear? Good.' She checked that the teachers were ready. Six hundred students looked around, seeing teachers lined up on both sides of the gym. 'Man,' Darla said quietly, 'they're making sure we can't have much fun, huh?' Mrs. McClure went on, 'The lowest bid is ten dollars, and you can go as high as you want, but no IOUs.' She pointed to her right.

If you win the auction, please go to the table near the north door after I announce the basket is sold. Winners and their helpers get the rest of the day off from school and don't have to do homework tonight. Thank you, teachers, for agreeing to this. Next, she put on her glasses and read from a small card. 'The first basket is from Jeffrey Bisho.' She asked Jeffrey to come to the front and not be shy. He slowly walked up. 'Jeffrey's basket has chicken salad sandwiches, noodles, small grapes, iced tea, and fortune cookies,' she announced, and complimented the food. She also mentioned that Jeffrey likes skateboarding, skiing, swimming, spending a day in the park, and watching old movies. She joked with him about the movies. Jeffrey tried to smile but seemed very embarrassed. Mrs. McClure then took off her glasses and started the auction. She quickly got offers of ten, twelve, fifteen, twenty, and twenty-five dollars. 'Sold!' she shouted to a girl in a purple shirt. I asked my friend Darla who the girl was. 'Her name is Tiffany, and she's in seventh grade,' Darla replied. I was surprised because I wouldn't have dared to bid last year, and I didn't remember the bids being so high. Darla looked at me and suggested I might bid this year, asking how much money I had. I felt nervous and explained that I didn't bring money on purpose, but my neighbor gave me some because she owed me for some eggs. 'Like what Bryce mentioned in the library?' Darla asked. 'Yes, exactly,' I replied, but then stopped talking when I saw Darla looking at me.

Why are you thinking of bidding on that boy? I don't really want to! But I've liked him since I was seven years old. Even though I know he's not brave and honest, and I shouldn't talk to him anymore, it's hard for me to think like that. Especially when Shelly Stalls likes him too. And now I have this money that I don't know what to do with! I get why you mentioned Shelly Stalls, but if you think that boy is not a good choice and you'll regret it, I can help you not spend your money. Give me the money, I'll keep it safe for you. No! No? I mean...I can take care of this. I need to deal with it. She shook her head, feeling sorry for me. I looked at the stage. The auction was moving so quickly! Soon it would be Bryce's turn. As the auction went on, I felt more and more confused about what to do. Then suddenly, everything in the gym went silent. You could hear a pin drop. Jon Trulock was standing next to Mrs. McClure, looking very embarrassed. Mrs. McClure was searching the crowd, feeling uncomfortable too. What happened? I asked Darla quietly. Nobody is bidding, she replied quietly. Do I hear ten dollars? Mrs. McClure shouted. Come on, everyone! The lunch is great. Strawberry tarts, roast beef, and Muenster cheese sandwiches... Oh no, I whispered to Darla. I can't believe I did this to him! You? What did you do? I voted for him! You probably weren't the only one... But why isn't anyone bidding on him? He's such a nice guy, I said. Darla agreed. That's when I knew what I had to do. I quickly raised my hand and shouted, Ten dollars! Ten dollars? Mrs. McClure asked. Did I hear ten dollars? I raised my hand higher and told Darla, Say twelve dollars. What? Say twelve dollars, I'll bid higher than you. No way! Darla, he can't be sold for just ten dollars, come on! Twelve dollars! Darla shouted, but she didn't raise her hand very high. Fifteen dollars! I shouted.

"Sixteen!" Darla shouted, and laughed at me. I quietly said, "Darla! I only have fifteen." Her eyes grew very big. I then shouted, "Eighteen!" and while holding her arm, I added, "But that's really all I have." Everyone was quiet for a bit, then someone shouted, "Eighteen going once! Eighteen going twice... Sold! for eighteen dollars." Darla laughed and said, "Wow, girl! That was exciting!" I agreed, "Yes, it was!" "Well, no dessert for you. It seems you spent all your money on something better." She hinted at the stage. "Are you going to the table as you should, or will you stay here to watch more?" I felt I had no choice. Before Mrs. McClure could talk about Bryce or his basket, Shelly shouted, "Ten!" Then from the middle of the gym, "Twenty!" shouted Miranda Humes, raising her hand high. They kept increasing their bids until Shelly shouted, "Sixty-two!" "I can't believe it," I whispered to Darla. "Sixty-two dollars! Go on, Miranda." "I think she's done. Shelly will win." "Sixty-two dollars going once!" Mrs. McClure announced, but before she could continue, someone from the back shouted, "A hundred!" Everyone was shocked and looked to see who it was. Darla whispered, "It's Jenny." "Atkinson?" I asked. Darla pointed, "Right there." Jenny was easy to see, standing tall in her usual basketball jersey number seven. "Wow," I whispered, "I had no idea." "Maybe she'll win it for you," Darla said smiling. "Who cares?" I laughed. "She outbid Shelly!" Mrs. McClure was excitedly talking about the high bid when suddenly there was a loud noise near Miranda. I saw Shelly's hair and thought there might be a fight. But instead, Shelly and Miranda together shouted, "One-twenty-two fifty!"

I stopped myself from crying out loud. 'What?' 'They are joining forces,' Darla whispered quietly. 'Oh no!' I looked towards Jenny. 'Let's go, Jenny!' But Darla shook her head and said, 'She's done,' and she really was done. Bryce went to Shelly and Miranda to collect $122.50 from them. It felt a bit odd to meet Jon and walk with him to the multi-purpose room for lunch. However, he was so kind, and seemed happy that I chose to spend time with him, that soon I felt comfortable and not silly anymore. It was just a normal lunch. It would have been easier if I wasn't sitting where I could see Bryce and his group of girls, but I tried my best to ignore them. Jon talked about a remote-controlled airplane he and his dad were building from scratch. He had been working on it for three months and they planned to test it that weekend. He shared a funny incident about messing up the wiring and almost causing a fire at home. I asked him to explain how the airplane worked because I didn't know much about it. So, I relaxed quite a bit and enjoyed my lunch with Jon. I was also glad I didn't choose Bryce. I would have felt so foolish! Seeing Shelly and Miranda around Bryce didn't upset me as much as I expected. In fact, they looked quite silly. Jon asked about my family, so I started telling him about my brothers and their music band when suddenly there was a loud noise from Bryce's table. Suddenly, Shelly and Miranda were on the floor, fighting and spreading food everywhere. Out of the blue, Bryce came to our table. He took my hand, pulled me aside, and whispered, 'Do you like him?' I was shocked. He took my other hand and asked again, 'Do you like him?' 'You mean Jon?'

"Yes!" I forgot what I said. He was staring into my eyes, holding my hands, and pulling me closer. My heart was beating fast, his eyes were closing, and his face was moving closer to mine. Right there, in front of all the other boys with baskets and their dates and the adults, he was about to kiss me.
He wanted to kiss me.
I got scared. I had been waiting for this kiss all my life, and now?

I pulled away and ran back to my table. When I sat down, Jon whispered, 'Did he just try to kiss you?' I turned my chair away from Bryce and whispered, 'Can we talk about something else? Anything else?' People were whispering and looking at me, and when Shelly Stalls came back from the bathroom, everyone got quiet. Her hair was messy. It was greasy and had bits of food in it. She looked at me so angrily, it seemed like she wanted to shoot lasers from her eyes.

Some adults guided her back to her seat, and then the whispering got even louder. Bryce didn't seem to care! He kept trying to talk to me, but a teacher would stop him or I would run away before he could speak.
When it was time to leave, I quickly said goodbye to Jon and rushed out. I couldn't get to my bike fast enough! I was the first to leave, and I rode home so fast, I thought I might explode.
Mrs. Stueby was in her yard watering flowers and tried to talk to me, but I just left my bike and ran inside. I didn't want to talk about roosters!
My mom heard me slamming doors and came to see me in my room. 'Julianna! What's wrong?' I turned on my bed to face her and cried, 'I am so confused! I don't know what to think or feel or do...!'

She sat next to me on the bed and touched my hair. 'What happened, sweetheart?' I paused, then threw my hands up. 'He tried to kiss me!' My mom tried to hide her smile and leaned closer. 'Who did?' 'Bryce!' She paused. 'But you liked him...' Then, the doorbell rang, and rang again. My mom stood up, but I held her arm. 'Don't answer it!' The bell rang again and someone knocked loudly. 'Mom, please! Don't open it. It might be him!' 'But sweetheart...' 'I don't like him anymore!' 'Since when?' 'Since last Friday, after dinner. If he disappeared after that dinner, I wouldn't care!' 'Why? What happened at dinner?' I lay back down. 'It's too complicated, Mom! I can't talk about it.' 'My,' she said. 'You sound like a teenager.' 'I'm sorry,' I said, feeling bad for upsetting her. I sat up. 'Mom, all those years I liked him? I didn't really know him. I only saw his beautiful eyes and his charming smile. But now I see he's not brave, he's sneaky. I need to stop liking him for his looks!' My mom sat back and crossed her arms. 'Well,' she said. 'This is interesting.' 'What do you mean?' She bit her cheek, thinking. Finally, she said, 'I shouldn't talk about it.' 'Why not?'

I shouldn't talk about it. Also, I feel there are things you're not comfortable talking about with me. We looked at each other quietly. Then I looked down and quietly said, 'When Chet and I were working in the yard, I told him we didn't own the house and about Uncle David. He must have told the family because I heard Bryce and his friend joking about Uncle David at school. I was very angry, but I didn't tell you because I thought you might think they invited us to dinner just because they felt sorry for us.' I told her she looked happy to be invited to dinner. Then I realized she has been happier since then. She held my hand and smiled, saying she has many reasons to be happy. Then she sighed and said she already knew they knew about Uncle David and it was okay to talk about him. I was surprised and asked how she knew. She said Patsy told her after the dinner. Patsy visited a lot that week because she was having a hard time. I asked why. Mom took a deep breath and said she thinks I can handle knowing something important, only because it's relevant. I waited. Mom said Patsy and Rick, Mr. and Mrs. Loski, were fighting a lot. I didn't understand. Mom quietly said Patsy is starting to see her husband's true nature after a long time and two kids. She said Patsy is going through something similar to what I am going through. Then the phone rang. Mom said it might be Dad calling to say he's working late.

While she was away, I thought about what Chet mentioned. He talked about someone who couldn't see the deeper side of things. Was he talking about his daughter? And how did this happen after being married for twenty years? When my mom returned, I asked without thinking, 'Is Dad working late tonight?' She replied, 'That wasn't your dad, it was Bryce.' I was shocked and sat up, 'Bryce is calling now? I've been his neighbor for six years and he never called before! Is he jealous or something?' 'Jealous? Of who?' she asked. So I told her everything from the beginning with Mrs. Stueby, through the events with Darla, the auction, the chaos with the furball, and ending with Bryce trying to kiss me in front of everyone. My mom laughed and clapped her hands. 'Mom, this is serious!' I said, trying to make her see my point. 'I understand, dear,' she replied. 'I don’t want to end up unhappy like Mrs. Loski!' 'You don’t have to marry him, Julianna. Just hear him out. He seems really eager to talk to you.' 'What does he want to talk about? He blamed Garrett for what he said about Uncle David, and I don’t trust him. He’s lied and hasn’t supported me. I don’t want anything to do with him. I need time to stop liking him after all these years.' My mom sat quietly, then said, 'People can change. Maybe he’s realized some things. And a boy who tries to kiss a girl in front of others isn't a coward. Maybe Bryce has more to him than you think.' Then she left me alone. My mom knew I needed space to think, but Bryce kept disturbing me. He called, knocked, and even tapped on my window. He was everywhere, bothering me.

I wanted to water my yard without being bothered. I didn't want to keep avoiding him at school or need Darla to help me escape. Why couldn't he understand that I wasn't interested in talking to him? What did he want to talk about anyway? Was it too much to ask to be left alone? That afternoon, I was reading in the front room with the curtains closed to hide from him, like I had been all week, when I heard a noise outside. I looked out and saw Bryce walking on my lawn. He was stepping heavily on my grass and had a spade with him! What was he planning to do? I jumped up from the couch, opened the door quickly, and bumped into my dad. 'Stop him!' I shouted. 'Relax, Julianna,' he replied, and gently pushed me back inside. 'I allowed him.' 'Allowed him to do what?' I rushed to the window. 'He's digging a hole.' 'Yes, I said he could.' 'But why?' 'I think it's a good idea,' he answered. 'But—' 'It won't harm your grass, Julianna. Let him do what he needs to do.' 'But what is he doing?' 'Just watch. You'll see.' It was hard to watch him dig up my lawn. The hole was huge! How could my dad let him do this? Bryce saw me watching and just nodded at me, without smiling or waving. He then brought over some soil, cut open the bag with the spade, and started filling the hole. He left and returned dragging a large plant with a root ball wrapped in burlap. The branches shook as he moved. My dad sat next to me and looked out the window too. 'A tree?' I asked quietly. 'He's planting a tree?' 'I would help him, but he wants to do it alone.' I almost asked if it was a certain type of tree, but I stopped myself. I didn't need to ask. My dad didn't need to answer. I recognized the shape of the leaves and the texture of the trunk. It was a sycamore tree.

I turned over on the sofa and sat down. A sycamore tree. Bryce finished planting the tree, watered it, cleaned up, and then went home. And I just sat there, not sure what to do next. I have been sitting here for hours, just looking out the window at the tree. It is small now, but it will grow bigger every day. In a hundred years, it will be taller than the houses. It will be very high! I can already see—it's going to be a big, beautiful tree. And I keep thinking, in a hundred years, will a child climb it like I climbed the one on Collier Street? Will she see what I saw? Will she feel what I felt? Will it change her life like it changed mine? I also keep thinking about Bryce. What is he trying to say to me? What is he thinking? I know he's at home because he sometimes looks out his window. A little while ago, he waved at me. And I waved back without thinking. So, maybe I should go over and thank him for the tree. Maybe we could sit on the porch and talk. I just realized that in all the years we've known each other, we've never really talked. Maybe my mother is right. Maybe there is more to Bryce Loski than I thought. Maybe it's time to really get to know him.
